PMA ≤ 29 weeks, PNA > 28 days.
PMA 30-36 weeks, PNA 0-14 days.
PMA 30-36 weeks, PNA > 14 days.
PMA 37-44 weeks, PNA 0-7 days.
PMA 37-44 weeks, PNA > 7 days.
PMA ≥ 45 weeks, All Postnatal Ages.

admin_details.notes: For IM administration, concentrations range from 66 mg/mL to 333 mg/mL.

ranges.notes: PMA ≤ 29 weeks, PNA 0-28 days.
PMA ≤ 29 weeks, PNA > 28 days.
PMA 30-36 weeks, PNA 0-14 days.
PMA 30-36 weeks, PNA > 14 days.
PMA 37-44 weeks, PNA 0-7 days.
PMA 37-44 weeks, PNA > 7 days.
PMA ≥ 45 weeks, All Postnatal Ages.

indications: Treatment of neonatal sepsis caused by susceptible gram-negative organisms including E. coli, H. influenzae, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, and Serratia.
Empirical treatment of sepsis in combination with ampicillin.
Synergistic treatment against Pseudomonas and Enterobacteriaceae when combined with an aminoglycoside.

iv.instructions: Administer via IV push over 3 to 5 minutes or as an IV infusion over 20 to 60 minutes. Maintain a final concentration not exceeding 20 mg/mL.
iv.preparation: For infusion: Reconstitute 1-g vial with at least 3 mL of Sterile Water for Injection (SWFI) and further dilute to ≤ 20 mg/mL. For bolus: Reconstitute 1-g or 2-g vial with 6 to 10 mL of SWFI.

im.instructions: Administer at concentrations ranging from 66 mg/mL to 333 mg/mL.
im.preparation: Dilute 1-g vial with at least 3 mL of an appropriate diluent; shake immediately and vigorously.

precautions.Hepatic: Monitor for elevations in serum transaminases (AST and ALT).
precautions.Renal: Eliminated primarily as unchanged drug via the kidneys; half-life is significantly extended in neonates (3 to 9 hours).
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Antibiotic has minimal effect on bowel flora due to lack of activity against anaerobic and gram-positive bacteria.
precautions.Metabolic: Contains 780 mg L-arginine per gram; ensure adequate glucose intake to prevent hypoglycemia. Monitor serum glucose one hour post-administration.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A synthetic monocyclic beta-lactam antibiotic that is bactericidal against aerobic gram-negative bacteria with virtually no activity against gram-positive or anaerobic organisms.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal (primarily as unchanged drug)

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Solutions ≤ 2% w/v or those prepared with SWFI/NS: Stable for 48 hours at room temperature (15-30°C) or 7 days if refrigerated (2-8°C). Solutions > 2% w/v (excluding SWFI/NS preparations) should be used promptly.

admin_details.notes: For intratracheal use only. Administer in 4 quarter-dose aliquots with the infant in different positions: 1) head/body down, head right; 2) head/body down, head left; 3) head/body up, head right; 4) head/body up, head left. Do not suction for 1 hour after dosing unless obstruction occurs. Use 5 French end-hole catheter.

ranges.duration: Up to 4 doses in first 48 hours of life
ranges.notes: Dose is 4 mL/kg/dose (equivalent to 100 mg/kg). First dose should be given as soon as possible, preferably within 15 minutes of birth. Obtain radiographic confirmation of RDS before administering additional doses. Do not give more frequently than every 6 hours.

admin_details.notes: For intratracheal use only. Administer in 4 quarter-dose aliquots with the infant in different positions. Do not suction for 1 hour after dosing unless obstruction occurs. Use 5 French end-hole catheter.

ranges.duration: Up to 4 doses in first 48 hours of life
ranges.notes: Dose is 4 mL/kg/dose (equivalent to 100 mg/kg). First dose should be given as soon as possible, preferably by 8 hours of age. Do not give more frequently than every 6 hours.

indications: Prevention and treatment of Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS) in premature infants.
Rescue therapy for infants with hypoxic respiratory failure due to secondary surfactant deficiency (e.g., meconium aspiration syndrome, sepsis, or pneumonia).
Prophylactic administration for preterm infants <30 weeks gestation requiring mechanical ventilation.

intratracheal.instructions: For intratracheal use only. Administer in four quarter-dose aliquots, repositioning the infant between each: 1) Head/body inclined 5-10° down, head right; 2) Head/body 5-10° down, head left; 3) Head/body 5-10° up, head right; 4) Head/body 5-10° up, head left. Insert a 5-French catheter into the ET tube (tip above carina). Inject each aliquot over 2-3 seconds; remove catheter and manually ventilate for ≥30 seconds or until stable. Do not suction for 1 hour post-dose unless airway obstruction occurs.
intratracheal.preparation: Warm vial at room temperature for ≥20 minutes or in hand for ≥8 minutes; do not use artificial warming. Swirl gently to redisperse; do not shake or filter. Withdraw dose using a ≥20 gauge needle into a plastic syringe. Prime a 5-French end-hole catheter with the drug, discarding excess to leave only the total dose in the syringe. Single-use vial only.

contraindications: No specific contraindications determined by the manufacturer.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Transient bradycardia may occur during administration; interrupt therapy and treat as necessary.
precautions.Respiratory: Rapid changes in oxygenation and lung compliance require immediate ventilator management; moist breath sounds/rales may occur post-administration.

serious: Intracranial hemorrhage (reported in specific clinical studies)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Modified natural bovine lung extract containing phospholipids, neutral lipids, fatty acids, and surfactant-associated proteins B and C. Complemented with colfosceril palmitate, palmitic acid, and tripalmitin to restore lung surface activity and surface tension.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Lipids enter endogenous surfactant pathways for reuse and recycling.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store at 2 to 8°C (36 to 46°F) and protect from light. Unopened vials warmed to room temperature may be returned to refrigeration within 24 hours (one-time only). Do not exceed 24 hours out of refrigeration.

admin_details.notes: Prepare eye with topical anesthesia (0.5% proparacaine or tetracaine) and ophthalmic antiseptic (5-10% povidone iodine). Administer using a sterile 30, 31, or 32-gauge 4-mm needle injected 0.75 to 2 mm posterior to the temporal limbus. Administer ophthalmic antibiotic drops for 7 days post-procedure.

ranges.notes: Most common dose used in studies. Typically 0.625 mg in 0.025 mL per eye. Retreatment may be needed after 55 weeks postmenstrual age or at a mean of 9.8 weeks (range 6-15 weeks) after initial injection.
Lower dose evaluated in uncontrolled trials. Typically 0.312 mg in 0.025 mL.
Lower dose evaluated in uncontrolled trials. Typically 0.16 mg in 0.025 mL.
Doses of 0.25 mg, 0.125 mg, 0.063 mg, and 0.031 mg were evaluated in a phase 1 dose de-escalation study for severe ROP type 1.

indications: Treatment of Type 1 Retinopathy of Prematurity (ROP) in infants with Zone I or posterior Zone II disease.
Reduction of refractive errors and risk of retinal detachment in specific ROP subgroups.
Off-label use in pediatric patients; safety and effectiveness not FDA established.

intravitreal.instructions: Administer via a sterile 30, 31, or 32-gauge 4-mm needle. Injection should be placed 0.75 mm to 2 mm posterior to the temporal limbus into the vitreous cavity.
intravitreal.preparation: Available as 25 mg/mL concentration. For smaller pediatric doses, dilutions ranging from 3.1 mg/mL to 12.5 mg/mL have been utilized.

iv.instructions: For patients undergoing surgery, withhold bevacizumab for at least 28 days prior to elective procedures. Do not resume for at least 28 days post-surgery and only once the surgical wound is fully healed.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Increased risk for severe (Grade 3/4) hypertension and congestive heart failure; decline in LVEF reported. May require therapy interruption or discontinuation.
precautions.Renal: Risk of nephrotic syndrome (sometimes fatal) and proteinuria; necessitates monitoring and potential discontinuation.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Serious and potentially fatal gastrointestinal perforation and fistulae reported, often within 50 days of treatment initiation; avoid in patients with evidence of bowel involvement or obstruction.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A recombinant humanized monoclonal IgG1 antibody that binds to vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), preventing its interaction with Flt1 and KDR receptors on endothelial cells, thereby inhibiting cell proliferation and new vessel formation.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Clearance: 0.23 L/day (adult IV)

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Preparation for intravitreal use requires sterile dilution; long-term systemic effects in infants remain unknown.

admin_details.notes: Give undiluted as a slow push for intermittent doses. For continuous infusion, 1 to 10 mcg/kg/hr has been used.

ranges.notes: Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit. Max effect seen at 0.035-0.04 mg/kg. Use lower dose for lung disease with normal renal function; higher dose for CHF or abnormal renal function.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit. Max effect seen at 0.035-0.04 mg/kg.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit. Max effect seen at 0.035-0.04 mg/kg.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit. Max effect seen at 0.035-0.04 mg/kg.
Continuous IV infusion (1 to 10 mcg/kg/hr) for critically-ill patients. Mean study dose: 5.7 mcg/kg/hr (0.0057 mg/kg/hr).

admin_details.notes: The intravenous formulation, diluted in sterile water and given orally, has been used successfully.

ranges.notes: Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit.
Doses up to 0.1 mg/kg studied, but doses > 0.05 mg/kg show no additional benefit.

indications: Promotion of diuresis in critically-ill pediatric patients to achieve negative fluid balance.
Treatment of heart failure or significant edema refractory to furosemide.
Supportive care for right-sided heart failure in neonates with pulmonary hypertension.

iv.instructions: Administer undiluted via slow intravenous injection over 1 to 2 minutes.
iv.preparation: May be administered undiluted at 0.25 mg/mL or prepared as a 0.125 mg/mL dilution by mixing equal parts (3 mL each) of the drug and preservative-free normal saline.

oral.instructions: The intravenous formulation can be administered orally for infants with congenital heart disease.
oral.preparation: Dilute the intravenous formulation in sterile water for oral use.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Monitor for arrhythmias, especially in patients with hypokalemia or those receiving digitalis.
precautions.Hepatic: Sudden electrolyte shifts may precipitate hepatic encephalopathy; initiation in patients with cirrhosis and ascites should occur in-patient with small doses.
precautions.Renal: Monitor for reversible elevations in BUN and creatinine; discontinue if progressive renal disease or oliguria develops.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Caution required in diarrheal states as they may exacerbate hypokalemia and electrolyte risks.

serious: Profound diuresis and severe water/electrolyte depletion
Ototoxicity (especially with high IV doses or renal impairment)
Kernicterus in neonates due to bilirubin displacement

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Loop diuretic that inhibits chloride reabsorption in the ascending limb of Henle's loop and inhibits tubular sodium transport, leading to increased excretion of sodium, chloride, potassium, calcium, and bicarbonate.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Significant hepatic metabolism (40%) via the cytochrome CYP pathway

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Refrigerated 0.125-mg/mL dilutions are stable for 24 hours. The drug is light-sensitive and discolors upon exposure.

admin_details.notes: Use only single-dose ampules and vials (preservative-free) for caudal or epidural anesthesia. Administer slowly in 3 to 5 mL incremental doses. Administer a test dose containing epinephrine and monitor effects before full dose. Avoid rapid injection of large volumes.

ranges.notes: Increased risk of methemoglobinemia in infants < 6 months. Dose varies by procedure and patient condition; use lowest dose to achieve effect. For continuous infusion: reduce rate in neonates at risk for seizures (e.g., pulmonary AV malformation, low seizure threshold, hyponatremia). Reduce dose in renal/hepatic impairment and acutely ill patients.

admin_details.notes: Check aspiration for blood or CSF prior to injection. Avoid rapid injection; use fractional (incremental) doses when possible.

ranges.notes: Increased risk of methemoglobinemia in infants < 6 months. Contraindicated for IV regional anesthesia (Bier Block). Head/neck administration requires close monitoring for systemic toxicity (seizures, respiratory depression). Reduce dose in renal/hepatic impairment and acutely ill patients.

indications: Production of local or regional anesthesia or analgesia for surgical, dental, and oral procedures.
Diagnostic and therapeutic procedures requiring anesthesia.
Epidural anesthesia via caudal or lumbar routes.
Peripheral nerve blocks including intercostal, interpleural, and dorsal penile blocks.
Spinal anesthesia (subarachnoid block).

epidural.instructions: Administer slowly in 3- to 5-mL incremental doses. Allow sufficient time between doses to detect signs of unintentional intravascular or intrathecal injection. Perform syringe aspiration before and during supplemental injections. Administer an epinephrine-containing test dose before the full dose and with subsequent doses if a catheter is present.
epidural.preparation: Use only preservative-free single-dose ampules or vials. Avoid rapid injection of large volumes.

spinal.instructions: Indicated for subarachnoid block. Monitor the level of anesthesia continuously as it is not always controllable. Avoid mixing or concurrent use with other local anesthetics.
spinal.preparation: Utilize hyperbaric (with dextrose) or isobaric solutions as indicated by patient age and procedure.

local_infiltration_and_peripheral_nerve_.instructions: Check aspiration for blood or cerebrospinal fluid prior to initial and subsequent injections. Avoid rapid injection of large volumes; use fractional/incremental doses when possible. Do not use solutions containing epinephrine near end-artery areas (e.g., digits, nose, penis).
local_infiltration_and_peripheral_nerve_.preparation: Summarize concentration based on procedure; avoid preservative-containing vials for specific regional blocks if required by protocol.

iv_regional_bier_block.instructions: Strictly contraindicated due to risk of systemic toxicity and cardiac arrest.

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to bupivacaine or amide-type anesthetics
Local infection at the site of lumbar puncture (spinal)
Severe hemorrhage, shock, or severe hypotension (spinal)

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of dose-related arrhythmias when combined with vasoconstrictors and potent inhalation anesthetics. Use caution in patients with heart block, hypotension, or hypertensive vascular disease (risk of necrosis in end-artery areas).
precautions.Hepatic: Increased risk of toxic plasma concentrations and accumulation in patients with severe hepatic disease; dosage adjustment and monitoring recommended.
precautions.Renal: Increased risk of toxic reactions in patients with renal impairment; clinical monitoring is recommended.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Potential for inadvertent trauma to the tongue, lips, and buccal mucosa following dental injections.

serious: Cardiac arrest and death (particularly with 0.75% concentration in obstetrics)
Permanent neurological injury (paralysis, weakness, or paresthesia)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Blocks the generation and conduction of nerve impulses by increasing the electrical excitation threshold, reducing the rate of action potential rise, and slowing impulse propagation.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily hepatic metabolism via glucuronic acid conjugation; 6% excreted unchanged in urine.

availability: Marcaine Spinal: 0.75% bupivacaine with 8.25% dextrose ampules
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store at 20-25°C. Discard unused portions of single-dose vials/ampules. Preservative-free solutions may be autoclaved once (121°C for 15 min). Epinephrine-containing products are light-sensitive and must not be autoclaved.

admin_details.notes: After administration sublingually, place a pacifier in the infant's mouth. For a volume of the dose greater than 0.5 mL, give in 2 administrations separated by at least 2 minutes.

ranges.notes: Initial starting dose is 15.9 mcg/kg/day (0.0159 mg/kg/day) divided every 8 hours. Maximum dose is 60 mcg/kg/day (0.06 mg/kg/day). Maximum number of up-titrations = 6. Titrate dose up in 25% increments. Taper in 10% decrements until the dose is 10% of the initial dose, then discontinue.

indications: Treatment of Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome (NAS).
Preferred over morphine and methadone for reducing duration of treatment and length of hospital stay in neonates (≥34 weeks gestation).

sublingual.instructions: Place a pacifier in the infant's mouth after administration. For dose volumes exceeding 0.5 mL, split the dose into two administrations separated by at least 2 minutes.
sublingual.preparation: A 0.075 mg/mL (75 mcg/mL) solution can be compounded by mixing buprenorphine for injection with 100% ethanol USP (30% of total volume) and simple syrup USP.

contraindications: Acute or severe bronchial asthma in unmonitored settings or without resuscitative equipment.
Known or suspected gastrointestinal obstruction, including paralytic ileus.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of QTc prolongation; avoid in patients with long QT syndrome. Potential for severe orthostatic hypotension, syncope, and circulatory shock.
precautions.Hepatic: Risk of hepatotoxicity, cytolytic hepatitis, and jaundice; caution in severe impairment or biliary dysfunction (Sphincter of Oddi spasm).
precautions.Renal: Caution in severe renal impairment, prostate hypertrophy, or urethral stricture.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: May obscure diagnosis of acute abdominal conditions; risk of severe constipation.

serious: Serotonin syndrome (especially with concomitant serotonergic drugs)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Partial mu-opioid receptor agonist and kappa-opioid receptor antagonist; provides narcotic analgesia with lower physical dependence potential than morphine.

pharmacokinetics.half_life: 11 hours in neonates; 31 to 35 hours in adults.
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Hepatic N-dealkylation primarily via CYP3A4 to norbuprenorphine, followed by glucuronidation.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Compounded solution is stable for 30 days at room temperature in glass bottles and 7 days at room temperature in syringes.

admin_details.notes: Administer maintenance dose over 10 minutes. Monitor serum caffeine concentrations in hepatic and renal impairment to prevent toxicity.

ranges.notes: FDA-approved dosage. Initial loading dose of 20 mg/kg (1 mL/kg) IV over 30 minutes required. Initiate maintenance dose 24 hours after loading dose.
Off-label dosage. Initial loading dose of 19 to 23 mg/kg (Median 20 mg/kg) IV required. Maintenance dose median: 8 mg/kg/day.

admin_details.notes: Monitor serum caffeine concentrations in hepatic and renal impairment to prevent toxicity.

ranges.notes: FDA-approved dosage. Initial loading dose of 20 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes required. Initiate maintenance dose 24 hours after loading dose.
Off-label dosage. Initial loading dose of 19 to 23 mg/kg (Median 20 mg/kg) IV required. Maintenance dose median: 8 mg/kg/day.

indications: FDA-approved for short-term treatment of apnea of prematurity in infants 28 to <33 weeks gestational age.
Reduction of bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) risk in very preterm infants (off-label high-dose therapy).
Prevention of reintubation failure in preterm infants.
Standard of care for pharmacological treatment of apnea of prematurity.
Off-label reduction of early acute kidney injury risk in preterm neonates.
Reduction of necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) and patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) ligation (high-dose therapy).

iv.instructions: Administer the loading dose over 30 minutes. Administer the maintenance dose over 10 minutes.
iv.preparation: Extemporaneous 20 mg/mL solution: Dissolve 10 g caffeine citrate in sterile water for injection (500 mL total), filter through 0.22 micron filter, and autoclave at 121°C for 15 minutes.

oral.instructions: Absorption is not affected by formula feeding. May be prepared as caffeine citrate or caffeine base solutions.
oral.preparation: Citrate solution (20 mg/mL): Dissolve 10 g caffeine citrate in sterile water and a 2:1 mixture of simple and cherry syrup (500 mL total). Base solution (10 mg/mL): Dissolve 2.5 g caffeine anhydrous powder in 250 mL water.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Use with caution in infants with cardiovascular disease due to risks of tachycardia and increased left ventricular output.
precautions.Hepatic: Use with caution in impaired hepatic function; monitor serum levels as dosage adjustments may be required due to immature enzyme metabolism.
precautions.Renal: Use with caution in impaired renal function; monitor serum levels and creatinine clearance as dosage adjustment may be needed.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Monitor closely for signs and symptoms of necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC).
precautions.Neurologic: Use with caution in infants with seizure disorders due to potential CNS stimulation.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Methylxanthine derivative that acts as a bronchial smooth muscle relaxant, CNS stimulant, and cardiac stimulant; primarily functions through antagonism of adenosine A1 and A2 receptor subtypes.

pharmacokinetics.half_life: 3 to 4 days in neonates; 5 hours by 9 months of age
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily renal; 86% excreted as unchanged drug in neonates.

availability: 20 mg/mL caffeine citrate (10 mg/mL base equivalent) preservative-free 3-mL single-use vials
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Commercial vials: Store at room temperature. Extemporaneous IV/Oral citrate solutions: 90 days refrigerated. Extemporaneous Oral base solution: 4 weeks refrigerated (dissolve crystals at room temperature).

admin_details.notes: Dosing is for elemental calcium. Formulations include: Calcium gluconate 10% (9.3 mg/mL elemental Ca), Calcium glubionate syrup (23 mg/mL elemental Ca), and Calcium carbonate 250 mg/mL suspension (100 mg/mL elemental Ca). Schedule doses around oral feedings.

ranges.notes: Total daily dose of 20 to 80 mg/kg/day elemental calcium divided every 6 to 12 hours (2 to 4 doses). Dose listed based on q6h interval.

admin_details.notes: Dosing is for elemental calcium. Formulations include: Calcium gluconate 10% (9.3 mg/mL), Calcium glubionate syrup (23 mg/mL), and Calcium carbonate (100 mg/mL).

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose: Initial 20 mg/kg/day elemental calcium (5 mg/kg q6h). Titrate as tolerated to max 70 to 80 mg/kg/day (approx 17.5-20 mg/kg q6h). Supplement with 10-20 mg/kg/day elemental phosphorus, titrating to 40-50 mg/kg/day.

indications: Treatment of non-acute hypocalcemia in neonates able to tolerate oral medications
Management of rickets in enterally fed preterm infants when nutrient maximization via human milk fortifiers or preterm formula is insufficient
Elemental calcium supplementation for very low birth weight infants (target intake: 150 to 220 mg/kg/day)

oral.instructions: Administer enterally; periodically monitor serum calcium, phosphorus, and vitamin D levels. Assess gastrointestinal tolerance during therapy. Maintain target 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentrations >20 ng/mL.
oral.preparation: Administer available suspension or syrup formulations; no specific reconstitution steps provided in text.

precautions.Gastrointestinal: High osmolarity of preparations (especially calcium glubionate syrup) may cause gastric irritation and diarrhea; use with extreme caution in infants at risk for necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC).

serious: Potential risk of necrotizing enterocolitis in vulnerable neonatal populations

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Provides elemental calcium essential for bone mineralization; oral absorption is primarily regulated by 1,25-dihydroxy Vitamin D.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Small intestine absorption; primary excretion route not specified

availability: Calcium carbonate: 250 mg/mL suspension (100 mg/mL elemental calcium) in 5-mL unit dose cups
Calcium glubionate: 360 mg/mL syrup (23 mg/mL elemental calcium) in 473 mL bottles

admin_details.notes: If initial dose is beneficial, may start an infusion at 20 to 50 mg/kg/hour titrated to blood pressure. Central line recommended.

ranges.notes: 0.2 mL/kg of calcium chloride 10% solution.

admin_details.notes: Use only for documented hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, or hypermagnesemia. A central line is recommended.

ranges.notes: 0.2 mL/kg of calcium chloride 10% solution.

admin_details.notes: Dose is per 100 mL of citrated blood exchanged.

ranges.notes: Equivalent to 0.33 mL of 10% calcium chloride per 100 mL blood.

admin_details.notes: Maximum rate is 1 mL/minute (100 mg/minute) of 10% solution.

ranges.notes: Experts recommend repeat doses every 4 to 6 hours.

admin_details.notes: Adjust to achieve daily urinary calcium excretion < 4 mg/kg/day.

ranges.notes: Maintenance dose: 148 to 296 mg/kg/day calcium chloride. Usually administered in 3 to 4 divided doses.

admin_details.notes: Adjust to achieve daily urinary calcium excretion < 4 mg/kg/day.

ranges.notes: Maintenance dose: 148 to 296 mg/kg/day calcium chloride. Usually administered in 3 to 4 divided doses.

admin_details.notes: Initial loading dose of 37 to 74 mg/kg (10 to 20 mg/kg elemental Ca) IV over 10-30 minutes required. Then maintenance.

ranges.notes: Continuous maintenance infusion. If calcium values are normal after 48 hours, decrease infusion by 50% for 24 hours then discontinue.

indications: Treatment of Calcium Channel Blocker (CCB) toxicity to improve heart rate, blood pressure, and conduction abnormalities.
Cardiac resuscitation in neonates with documented hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, hypermagnesemia, or CCB toxicity.
Acute management of hyperkalemia to stabilize cardiac cellular membranes.
Management of hypocalcemia (ionized calcium < 4.4 mg/dL in term infants or < 4 mg/dL in preterm infants).
FDA-approved for prompt increase of plasma calcium concentrations in emergency conditions.

iv.instructions: Administer slowly through a large vein or central/deep vein. Rate must not exceed 1 mL/min of 100 mg/mL solution. Warm to body temperature before administration if possible. Avoid high concentrations reaching the heart to prevent cardiac syncope.
iv.preparation: May be administered undiluted over 30 to 60 minutes or diluted 1:1 with D5W and administered over 10 to 30 minutes.

io.instructions: Intraosseous administration is acceptable in emergency cardiac arrest settings.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Rapid administration is associated with bradycardia, cardiac arrest, and cardiac syncope; use with extreme caution in patients receiving digitalis due to additive effects.
precautions.Renal: Risk of aluminum toxicity with prolonged parenteral administration, particularly in premature neonates or those with impaired renal function.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Bolus infusions via Umbilical Arterial Catheter (UAC) are associated with intestinal bleeding.

common: Metabolic acidosis (more frequent than with calcium gluconate)
Local irritation at the injection site
serious: Fatal crystalline deposits in lungs and kidneys when coadministered with ceftriaxone in neonates
Severe tissue necrosis and sloughing following extravasation or accidental perivascular injection
Intestinal bleeding and lower-extremity tissue necrosis (associated with UAC bolus)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Calcium is essential for nervous and muscular system integrity, cardiac function, and blood coagulation. In hyperkalemia, it stabilizes cardiac membranes without affecting potassium levels.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: 80% Fecal (as insoluble salts); 20% Renal

availability: Calcium chloride 10% injection (27 mg/mL elemental calcium; 1.36 mEq/mL)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store at room temperature; stable indefinitely.

admin_details.notes: Administer via continuous infusion. Titrate to blood pressure. Administer via secure IV line to avoid calcinosis cutis and tissue necrosis. Do not administer ceftriaxone and calcium gluconate via Y-site.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose of 60 mg/kg (max 6 g) required. Titrate maintenance to blood pressure.

admin_details.notes: Slow IV push/IO. Max rate 100 mg/min. Administer via secure IV line. Do not mix with ceftriaxone.

ranges.notes: Repeat dose as necessary for clinical effect. Indicated for documented hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, hypermagnesemia, or CCB overdose.

admin_details.notes: Administer bolus slowly; do not exceed 100 mg/min. Flush line thoroughly if ceftriaxone is used sequentially.

ranges.notes: Equivalent to 430-860 mg/kg/day divided every 6 to 8 hours. Adjust to achieve urinary calcium excretion < 4 mg/kg/day.

admin_details.notes: Equivalent to 40-80 mg/kg/day elemental calcium.

ranges.notes: Equivalent to 430-860 mg/kg/day divided every 6 to 8 hours.

admin_details.notes: Continuous infusion. Do not exceed 100 mg/min for boluses. Avoid intra-arterial infusion.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose via continuous infusion. Initial loading dose of 100-200 mg/kg IV over 10-30 minutes required. If calcium is normal after 48h, decrease infusion by 50% for 24h then discontinue.

admin_details.notes: Daily maintenance in PN. 1 mEq = 215 mg Calcium Gluconate.

ranges.notes: Calculated from 2-4 mEq/kg/day for preterm neonates.
Calculated from 0.5-4 mEq/kg/day for full-term neonates.

admin_details.notes: Administer 1 mL (100 mg) of 10% calcium gluconate per 100 mL of citrated blood exchanged.

ranges.notes: Dose is per 100 mL of citrated blood exchanged.

indications: Treatment of calcium channel blocker (CCB) toxicity to improve heart rate and blood pressure.
Stabilization of cardiac cellular membrane in hyperkalemia (does not lower potassium levels).
Management of acute symptomatic hypocalcemia in pediatric and neonatal patients.
Treatment of documented hypocalcemia, hypermagnesemia, or CCB toxicity during neonatal cardiac resuscitation (not for routine use).

iv.instructions: Administer boluses slowly over 10 to 30 minutes; do not exceed a rate of 100 mg/min. Use a secure IV line to prevent calcinosis cutis and tissue necrosis. Avoid intra-arterial administration. For umbilical venous catheters, ensure the tip is not positioned close to or in the heart. Do not use Y-site administration with ceftriaxone. If used sequentially with ceftriaxone, flush lines thoroughly with a compatible fluid.
iv.preparation: Dilute to 10–50 mg/mL for bolus administration and 5.8–10 mg/mL for continuous infusion.

contraindications: Coadministration with ceftriaxone sodium in neonates (risk of ceftriaxone-calcium precipitation)

precautions.Cardiovascular: Rapid administration may cause vasodilation, hypotension, bradycardia, syncope, arrhythmias, and cardiac arrest.
precautions.Hepatic: Liver metabolism is not required for the release of free calcium (relevant for selection over calcium chloride in certain cases).
precautions.Renal: Risk of aluminum toxicity with prolonged use or in patients with impaired kidney function, potentially leading to CNS and bone toxicity.

serious: Crystalline deposits in lungs and kidneys (neonates coadministered ceftriaxone)
Tissue necrosis and calcinosis cutis following extravasation

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Dissociates into ionized calcium in the plasma, directly increasing serum ionized calcium levels for physiological utilization.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Not directly metabolized; ionized calcium and gluconate are normal body fluid constituents.

availability: 100 mg/mL (10%) injection solution (implied by dilution instructions)

admin_details.notes: For intratracheal administration only. Warming not necessary. Administer via side-port adapter (2 aliquots of 1.5 mL/kg) or 5 French feeding tube (4 equal aliquots). Use specific positioning (prone, supine, right, left lateral) for even distribution.

ranges.notes: Dose corresponds to 3 mL/kg. Initial dose should be given as soon as possible, preferably within 30 minutes after birth. May be repeated every 12 hours. Repeat doses may be given as early as 6 hours apart (up to 4 doses total) if infant is intubated and requires >= 30% FiO2.

admin_details.notes: For intratracheal administration only. Warming not necessary. Administer via side-port adapter (2 aliquots of 1.5 mL/kg) or 5 French feeding tube (4 equal aliquots). Use specific positioning (prone, supine, right, left lateral) for even distribution.

ranges.notes: Dose corresponds to 3 mL/kg. Initial dose should be given as soon as possible, preferably within 2 hours after birth. May be repeated every 12 hours. Repeat doses may be given as early as 6 hours apart (up to 4 doses total) if infant is intubated and requires >= 30% FiO2.

indications: Prevention of respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) in premature infants <29 weeks gestational age at high risk.
Treatment of RDS in infants <72 hours of age with clinical and radiological confirmation requiring endotracheal intubation.
Rescue therapy for hypoxic respiratory failure due to secondary surfactant deficiency (e.g., meconium aspiration syndrome, sepsis, or pneumonia).

intratracheal.instructions: Administer 3 mL/kg total dose via side-port adapter or 5 French feeding tube. Side-port method: Instill in two 1.5 mL/kg aliquots with patient in side-dependent positions; ventilate for 20-30 breaths per aliquot. Feeding tube method: Instill in four equal aliquots (prone, supine, right, and left lateral positions) with 0.5-2 minutes of mechanical ventilation between doses. Monitor for reflux and airway obstruction.
intratracheal.preparation: Warming is not required. Inspect for off-white color and normal flecks/foaming. Gently swirl the vial to resuspend contents; do not shake. Single-use vials only; discard residual drug.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of transient bradycardia and cyanosis during administration.
precautions.Respiratory: Potential for transient surfactant reflux, airway obstruction, and requirement for manual ventilation; requires close monitoring of oxygenation and ventilatory support.
precautions.Neurological: Increased risk of intraventricular hemorrhage and periventricular leukomalacia observed in clinical trials compared to some synthetic surfactants.

common: Reflux of surfactant into endotracheal tube (21%)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Natural calf-lung extract containing phospholipids and surfactant-associated proteins (B and C) that reduces alveolar surface tension to stabilize the alveoli and improve pulmonary gas exchange.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primary routes of excretion not specified; endogenously processed by pulmonary pathways.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store refrigerated at 2-8°C (36-46°F) and protect from light; 3-mL vials must be stored upright. Unopened vials warmed to room temperature once may be returned to the refrigerator within 24 hours for future use (limit one warming cycle).

admin_details.notes: Administer 1 hour before feeding; food decreases absorption. Extreme care warrants in term neonates and questions use in preterm neonates due to significant decrease in creatinine clearance.

ranges.notes: Initial dose: 0.01 mg/kg/dose every 12 hours. Incrementally increase dose. Adjust dose and interval (range: 6 to 24 hours) based on response.

admin_details.notes: Administer 1 hour before feeding; food decreases absorption. Extreme care warrants in term neonates and questions use in preterm neonates due to significant decrease in creatinine clearance.

ranges.notes: Based on recommended 0.4 to 1.6 mg/kg/day orally divided every 8 hours.

admin_details.notes: Administer 1 hour before feeding; food decreases absorption. Extreme care warrants in term neonates and questions use in preterm neonates due to significant decrease in creatinine clearance.

ranges.notes: Based on retrospective study. Dose range 1 to maximum 1.5 mg/kg/day orally divided every 8 hours.

indications: Afterload reduction in patients with congestive heart failure
Treatment of moderate to severe hypertension

oral.instructions: Administer 1 hour before feeding, as food significantly decreases drug absorption. Frequent blood pressure monitoring is required, especially after the initial dose.
oral.preparation: Can be compounded into oral solutions/suspensions using tablets and distilled water. Addition of ascorbic acid or sodium ascorbate is recommended to decrease oxidation. Do not filter the final preparation.

reconstitution_table.ingredients: Two 50-mg tablets, 500 mg ascorbic acid, distilled water to 100 mL
Two 50-mg tablets, 500 mg sodium ascorbate injection, distilled water to 100 mL
6.25 mg captopril (1/2 of 12.5-mg tablet), 1000 mg sodium ascorbate, sterile water to 200 mL
reconstitution_table.stability: 56 days refrigerated; 14 days at room temperature

contraindications: Unilateral renal artery stenosis in a solitary kidney

precautions.Cardiovascular: Neonates exhibit high sensitivity; risk of profound blood pressure drops and reduced cerebral blood flow.
precautions.Renal: Risk of acute renal failure in patients with renal artery stenosis; documented decreases in CrCl in preterm and term neonates with cardiovascular disease.

common: Hyperkalemia (particularly when used with potassium-sparing diuretics or supplements)
serious: Significant decrease in cerebral and renal blood flow in premature infants

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor that blocks conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. Reduces aldosterone levels, increases renin activity, and prevents bradykinin breakdown, resulting in reduced vascular resistance without reflex tachycardia.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Aqueous solutions degrade rapidly; stability is highly variable depending on pH, vehicle, and preservatives. Compounded solutions generally last 14–56 days refrigerated depending on the formulation.

admin_details.notes: Disperse tablets in water; do not swallow whole or crushed. Administer immediately before meals or feedings. Use only water for mixing. For NG tube: Draw into catheter-tip syringe, administer immediately, and flush with 1 to 2 mL of water. Administer with other ammonia-lowering therapies (e.g., alternate pathway medications, hemodialysis, and protein restriction).

ranges.notes: Usual total daily dosage: 100 to 250 mg/kg/day divided into 2 to 4 doses (dose range here reflects q12h). Round to the nearest 100 mg. Titrate dosage to maintain plasma ammonia level within normal range for age.

admin_details.notes: Maintenance treatment. Disperse tablets in water; do not swallow whole or crushed. Administer immediately before meals or feedings. For NG tube: Flush with 1 to 2 mL of water after administration.

ranges.notes: Usual total daily dosage: 10 to 100 mg/kg/day divided into 2 to 4 doses (dose range here reflects q12h). Round to the nearest 100 mg. Titrate to maintain normal plasma ammonia levels.

admin_details.notes: Disperse tablets in water; do not swallow whole or crushed. Administer immediately before meals or feedings. Give with other ammonia lowering therapies (e.g., IV glucose, insulin, L-carnitine, protein restriction, and dialysis).

ranges.notes: Total daily dose 150 mg/kg/day. Round up to the next multiple of 50 mg. Continue until ammonia level is less than 50 micromol/L (maximum duration of 7 days).

indications: Adjunctive therapy for the treatment of acute hyperammonemia due to N-acetylglutamate synthase (NAGS) deficiency from birth.
Maintenance therapy for chronic hyperammonemia due to NAGS deficiency.
Adjunctive therapy for acute hyperammonemia due to propionic acidemia (PA) or methylmalonic acidemia (MMA) in pediatric patients.
Off-label use for acute ammonia reduction in branched-chain organic acidemias (e.g., isovaleric acidemia).

oral.instructions: Administer immediately before meals or feedings. Swallow the mixture immediately. If tablet pieces remain in the cup, add more water and repeat until the entire dose is consumed. Use of an oral syringe is permitted; if particles remain, flush with 1-2 mL of water and repeat until clear.
oral.preparation: Disperse tablets in water only; do not swallow whole or crush. Tablets will not dissolve completely; undissolved particles are expected.

ng_tube.instructions: Administer immediately via NG or G-tube using a catheter-tip syringe. Flush the tube with 1 to 2 mL of water immediately after administration; repeat as necessary until no tablet pieces remain in the syringe or tube.
ng_tube.preparation: Disperse tablets in water only; do not swallow whole or crush. Ensure mixture is drawn into a catheter-tip syringe immediately after dispersion.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Synthetic analogue of N-acetylglutamate (NAG) that serves as an essential activator of carbamoyl phosphate synthetase 1 (CPS 1), the first enzyme in the urea cycle, thereby facilitating ammonia detoxification.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily fecal (60% as unchanged drug) and renal (9% as unchanged drug)

availability: 200 mg tablet for oral suspension (functionally scored into 4 equal portions)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store unopened containers under refrigeration (2-8°C). After opening, store at room temperature (15-30°C), protect from moisture, and do not refrigerate. Discard one month after initial opening.

admin_details.notes: Administer by slow IV infusion. Do not dilute in dextrose-containing solutions. The recommended concentration is 0.5 mg/mL and must not exceed 0.5 mg/mL.

ranges.duration: 2 weeks after documented clearance of Candida from the bloodstream and resolution of symptoms

admin_details.notes: Administer by slow IV infusion. Do not dilute in dextrose-containing solutions.

ranges.duration: At least 3 weeks and for at least 2 weeks following resolution of symptoms
ranges.notes: Alternate therapy.

admin_details.notes: Administer by slow IV infusion. Do not dilute in dextrose-containing solutions.

ranges.duration: Based on presence of deep-tissue foci and clinical response; in patients with candidemia, treat until 2 weeks after a positive blood culture
ranges.notes: Specifically for critically-ill patients.

indications: Empirical therapy for presumed fungal infections in febrile, neutropenic patients.
Treatment of candidemia and specific Candida infections including intra-abdominal abscesses, peritonitis, and pleural space infections.
Treatment of esophageal candidiasis.
Salvage therapy for invasive aspergillosis in patients refractory to or intolerant of other therapies.
Alternative/salvage therapy for neonatal candidiasis when first-line agents (Amphotericin B, Fluconazole) are inappropriate or resisted.

iv.instructions: Administer by slow IV infusion over approximately 1 hour. Final concentration must not exceed 0.5 mg/mL. Monitor IV site for signs of irritation.
iv.preparation: 1. Equilibrate refrigerated vial to room temperature. 2. Aseptically reconstitute 50 mg or 70 mg vial with 10.8 mL Normal Saline or Sterile Water for Injection. 3. Mix gently until dissolved. 4. Further dilute the required dose in a compatible solution (NS, 1/2 NS, 1/4 NS, or LR). 5. Do not use dextrose-containing diluents.

contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to caspofungin or any component of the formulation.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of hypotension and histamine-mediated reactions including facial swelling and sensation of warmth.
precautions.Hepatic: Risk of hepatotoxicity, especially when used with cyclosporine; monitoring of liver function tests and potential dose adjustment for impairment required.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Potential for diarrhea.
precautions.Dermatologic: Risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis; discontinue immediately at first sign of rash.
precautions.Immunologic: Risk of anaphylaxis and histamine-mediated reactions (angioedema, pruritus, bronchospasm).

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: An echinocandin antifungal that inhibits the synthesis of β-(1,3)-D-glucan, a critical component of fungal cell walls. Exhibits fungicidal activity against Candida and fungistatic activity against Aspergillus.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily hepatic metabolism via O-methyltransferase; not metabolized by CYP450 system.

availability: 50 mg single-use vial (white to off-white powder cake)
70 mg single-use vial (white to off-white powder cake)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted solution in vial: 1 hour at room temperature. Diluted infusion solution: 24 hours at room temperature or 48 hours refrigerated.

admin_details.notes: For IV bolus injection, inject slowly over 3 to 5 minutes at a concentration of 100 mg/mL. For IV infusion, dilute to 5 to 20 mg/mL.

indications: Perioperative infection prophylaxis in neonates
Treatment of urinary tract infections
Treatment of soft tissue infections caused by susceptible organisms (e.g., penicillin-resistant Staph. aureus, Klebsiella, and Proteus)
Infective endocarditis (directed therapy for Streptococci, Staphylococci, Gram-negative enteric bacilli, and HACEK group)
Sepsis prophylaxis during PICC line removal in preterm infants

iv.instructions: Administer via direct bolus injection slowly over 3 to 5 minutes, or via intermittent/continuous infusion.
iv.preparation: For IV bolus, prepare at a concentration of 100 mg/mL. For infusion, dilute reconstituted solution to a final concentration of 5 to 20 mg/mL.

im.instructions: Administer via intramuscular injection.
im.preparation: Recommended concentration of 225 mg/mL; maximum allowable concentration is 330 mg/mL.

precautions.Renal: Drug is renally excreted as unchanged drug; monitoring may be required in renal impairment.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Bactericidal first-generation cephalosporin; active against many gram-positive and select gram-negative organisms. Susceptible to inactivation by beta-lactamase and exhibits poor CNS penetration.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal (excreted as unchanged drug)

admin_details.notes: Standard concentrations: 40 mg/mL and 100 mg/mL (IV push).

ranges.notes: Term and preterm infants.
Term and preterm infants.

admin_details.notes: To reduce pain at IM injection site, cefepime may be mixed with 1% lidocaine without epinephrine.

ranges.notes: Term and preterm infants.
Term and preterm infants.

admin_details.notes: Includes severe infections due to Pseudomonas aeruginosa or Enterobacter spp. Standard concentrations: 40 mg/mL and 100 mg/mL (IV push).

ranges.notes: Specific for Meningitis or severe Pseudomonas/Enterobacter infections.

indications: Treatment of serious infections caused by susceptible gram-negative organisms including E. coli, H. influenzae, Enterobacter, Klebsiella, Morganella, Neisseria, Serratia, and Proteus species.
Specific efficacy against Pseudomonas aeruginosa resistant to 3rd generation cephalosporins.
Treatment of serious infections caused by susceptible gram-positive organisms including S. pneumoniae, S. pyogenes, S. agalactiae, and S. aureus.
Management of neonatal sepsis (early-onset and late-onset).
Empirical and organism-directed therapy for infective endocarditis (native and prosthetic valves).
Treatment of culture-negative endocarditis in pediatric patients.

iv.instructions: Administer as an IV infusion over 30 minutes. Standard concentrations for healthcare systems are often set at 40 mg/mL and 100 mg/mL (IV push).
iv.preparation: Reconstitute vials with a compatible diluent (Sterile Water for Injection, NS, or D5W). For final infusion, further dilute the reconstituted solution to a concentration between 1 and 40 mg/mL.

im.instructions: Administer by deep IM injection. To minimize localized pain, the drug may be mixed with 1% lidocaine without epinephrine.
im.preparation: Reconstitute with compatible diluent (Sterile Water, NS, D5W, 0.5% or 1% Lidocaine, or Bacteriostatic Water).

precautions.Hepatic: Risk of elevated hepatic transaminases.
precautions.Renal: Increased risk of acute kidney injury (reported at 16.2% in neonatal studies), especially when used concomitantly with other nephrotoxic agents like vancomycin and gentamicin.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Potential for diarrhea.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Fourth-generation cephalosporin providing rapid cell wall penetration of Gram-negative pathogens, enhanced stability against beta-lactamase hydrolysis, and high affinity for penicillin-binding proteins.

pharmacokinetics.half_life: Approximately 2 hours (in infants > 2 months of age)
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily excreted unchanged in the urine

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted solutions and diluted infusion solutions are stable for 24 hours at room temperature and 7 days when refrigerated.

indications: Disseminated gonococcal infections and gonococcal scalp abscesses
Infective endocarditis caused by Streptococci, Staphylococci, Gram-negative enteric bacilli, or HACEK group organisms
Empiric and pathogen-specific treatment of neonatal meningitis
Sepsis caused by susceptible gram-negative organisms (e.g., E. coli, H. influenzae, Klebsiella)

im.instructions: Administer via deep intramuscular injection.

iv_push.instructions: Administer over 3 to 5 minutes. Avoid rapid bolus injection via central venous catheters due to risk of life-threatening arrhythmias.

intermittent_iv_infusion.preparation: Dilute to a concentration of 10 to 40 mg/mL.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Rapid bolus injection via central venous catheter has resulted in life-threatening arrhythmias.
precautions.Renal: Use caution in patients with renal insufficiency; dose reductions are recommended for CrCL less than 20 mL/min/1.73 m².
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Use cautiously in patients with a history of colitis; risk of Clostridium difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) ranging from mild to fatal.

other_precautions: Extravasation may cause extensive tissue damage requiring surgical intervention.
Potential for hematologic effects including leukopenia, neutropenia, and rare bone marrow failure.
False-positive urine glucose reactions may occur with copper reduction tests.
Risk of developing drug-resistant bacteria if used without a proven bacterial infection.

serious: Increased risk of candidiasis in extremely low birth weight infants (<1000g)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Third-generation cephalosporin that exerts bactericidal activity by disrupting bacterial cell wall synthesis.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal excretion: 20% to 36% as unchanged drug and 15% to 25% as the active metabolite desacetylcefotaxime.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Not specified; monitor periodic CBC during therapy.

ranges.notes: Calculated from total daily dose of 90 to 100 mg/kg/day.

indications: Treatment of neonatal skin, intra-abdominal, and urinary tract infections.
Effective against anaerobic bacteria including Bacteroides fragilis.

iv.instructions: Administer as an intermittent IV infusion at a concentration of 10 to 40 mg/mL over 15 to 60 minutes.

precautions.Hepatic: Transient elevation of hepatic transaminases reported in <3% of patients.
precautions.Renal: Primary route of excretion (85-90% unchanged drug); half-life is significantly prolonged in neonates compared to adults.

serious: Severe overdose: tachypnea, pallor, hypotonia, and metabolic acidosis

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Second-generation bactericidal cephalosporin that inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins; features enhanced anaerobic activity and resistance to β-lactamase.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal (85% to 90% as unchanged drug)

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Prepared solution is stable for 18 hours at room temperature or 7 days under refrigeration.

admin_details.notes: IV push over 3 to 5 minutes (max concentration 100-200 mg/mL) or intermittent IV infusion over 30 minutes (concentration 1 to 50 mg/mL).

indications: Treatment of neonatal meningitis and sepsis caused by gram-negative organisms (E. coli, H. influenzae, Neisseria, Klebsiella, Proteus, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa).
Empirical and organism-directed therapy for infective endocarditis (native or prosthetic valves).
Treatment of infections caused by Streptococci (viridans, groups A, B, C, G, and D).
Treatment of Staphylococcal infections (S. aureus and coagulase-negative staphylococci), including penicillin-resistant strains.
Treatment of HACEK group infections (Haemophilus, Aggregatibacter, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, and Kingella species).
Treatment of infections caused by gram-negative enteric bacilli.

iv.instructions: Administer via IV push over 3 to 5 minutes or as an intermittent IV infusion over 30 minutes. Use a vented needle during reconstitution to manage carbon dioxide gas formation and prevent leaking.
iv.preparation: For IV push: Reconstitute 500-mg vial with 10 mL sterile water (50 mg/mL); max concentration 100-200 mg/mL. For intermittent infusion: Dilute to a concentration of 1 to 50 mg/mL.

im.instructions: Administer via deep IM injection into a large muscle mass; reserved for less serious infections.
im.preparation: Reconstitute 500-mg vial with 2.2 mL of 1% lidocaine (without epinephrine) or Sterile Water to reach a concentration of 200 mg/mL.

precautions.Hepatic: May cause elevated hepatic transaminases.
precautions.Renal: Serum half-life is significantly prolonged in patients with renal impairment; dosage adjustments may be required.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Increased risk of candidiasis in extremely low birth weight infants (<1000 g) if treatment starts by day 3 of life; potential for diarrhea.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Bactericidal third-generation cephalosporin that inhibits enzymes responsible for bacterial cell-wall synthesis; exhibits specific activity against Pseudomonas species.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: 80% to 90% excreted unchanged in the urine

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted solutions are stable for 12 hours at room temperature or 3 days when refrigerated.

admin_details.notes: Use caution in neonates due to risk of kernicterus. Do not mix with calcium-containing solutions in the same IV line; precipitation may occur. Avoid administration of calcium-containing solutions or products within 48 hours of the last administration of ceftriaxone.

admin_details.notes: Use caution in neonates due to risk of kernicterus. Do not mix with calcium-containing solutions in the same IV line. Avoid calcium for 48 hours after dose.
