indications: Treatment of gonococcal infections in neonates including ophthalmia neonatorum, disseminated infection, and scalp abscess
Prophylaxis of ophthalmia neonatorum when erythromycin ointment is unavailable
Treatment of Infective Endocarditis caused by Streptococci, Staphylococci, Gram-negative enteric bacilli, and HACEK group organisms
Empiric and directed therapy for nontyphoidal Salmonella infections
Treatment of Typhoid fever (Salmonella typhi or Salmonella paratyphi)
Second-line therapy for Shigella infections resistant to ciprofloxacin
Treatment of neonatal sepsis caused by susceptible gram-negative organisms (E. coli, Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, H. influenzae)

iv.instructions: Administer via infusion over 60 minutes. Recommended concentration range is 10 to 40 mg/mL. Do not mix with calcium-containing solutions in the same line. Avoid calcium-containing products within 48 hours of the last dose.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute powder with Sterile Water for Injection, D5W, or D10W to a concentration of 100 mg/mL. To achieve a 40 mg/mL concentration, add 6.2 mL of diluent to a 250 mg vial.

im.instructions: Administer into a large muscle mass. Reconstitution with lidocaine is recommended to reduce injection site pain.
im.preparation: Reconstitute a 250 mg vial with 0.9 mL of 1% lidocaine (without epinephrine) to reach a final concentration of 250 mg/mL. Concentrations up to 350 mg/mL are utilized.

contraindications: Neonates with hyperbilirubinemia (risk of displacement of bilirubin and kernicterus)
Concurrent administration with IV calcium-containing solutions (e.g., parenteral nutrition) in neonates
Use of lidocaine as a diluent for intravenous administration

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of fatal cardiorespiratory arrest and crystalline precipitation in vascular beds (lungs/kidneys) when administered concurrently with calcium in infants.
precautions.Hepatic: Dosage adjustment required if both hepatic and renal dysfunction coexist; risk of biliary stasis, sludge, and pancreatitis secondary to biliary obstruction.
precautions.Renal: Risk of ceftriaxone-calcium precipitates in the urinary tract (urolithiasis) and post-renal acute renal failure; dose adjustment needed if hepatic impairment is also present.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Reported cases of Clostridium difficile-associated diarrhea; high risk of gallbladder pseudolithiasis in pediatric patients.

serious: Increased risk of candidiasis in extremely low birth weight infants

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A third-generation cephalosporin antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis and distributes widely into body fluids and tissues, including the CSF and bile.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Eliminated unchanged via biliary (40%) and renal mechanisms.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: IV reconstituted solution (100 mg/mL): 2 days at room temperature, 10 days refrigerated. IM reconstituted solution (250 mg/mL): 24 hours at room temperature, 3 days refrigerated. Potency is maintained even if the solution darkens.

admin_details.notes: Oral preparation should be diluted or administered after a feeding to reduce gastric irritation. Note: Oral and rectal products are no longer manufactured in the US. Due to long half-life, unpredictable responses, and reported deaths, chloral hydrate is a poor choice as a sedating agent for procedures.

ranges.notes: Per dose basis. Often limited by poor safety profile.

admin_details.notes: The oral preparation may be given rectally. Note: Oral and rectal products are no longer manufactured in the US. Due to long half-life, unpredictable responses, and reported deaths, chloral hydrate is a poor choice as a sedating agent for procedures.

ranges.notes: Per dose basis. Oral preparation used for rectal administration.

indications: Short-term sedative and hypnotic use
Note: Lacks analgesic properties; may cause paradoxical excitement in patients experiencing pain

oral.instructions: Administer after feeding to minimize gastric irritation.
oral.preparation: Dilute the oral preparation prior to administration.

rectal.instructions: The oral preparation may be administered rectally.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of myocardial depression and cardiac arrhythmias, particularly with repeated dosing or overdose.
precautions.Hepatic: Contraindicated in significant impairment; metabolites compete for hepatic conjugation, potentially leading to indirect hyperbilirubinemia.
precautions.Renal: Contraindicated in significant impairment; active metabolites are excreted renally and may accumulate.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Oral route may cause significant gastric irritation; potential for ileus with toxicity.

common: Bradycardia (specifically in former premature infants for up to 24 hours post-dose)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Rapidly converted by alcohol dehydrogenase into the active metabolite trichloroethanol (TCEt), which provides sedative effects.

pharmacokinetics.half_life: TCEt: 8 to 64 hours (neonates); TCA: several days (neonates)
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal excretion following hepatic glucuronidation

availability: Oral and rectal products are no longer manufactured in the US
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Metabolites (TCEt and TCA) have long half-lives in neonates and accumulate with repeated dosing.

admin_details.notes: Avoid in neonates unless serum concentration monitoring is used due to risk of gray baby syndrome. NIOSH safety: use double gloves, protective gown, and closed system drug transfer device.

ranges.notes: Maintenance dose. In very low-birth weight neonates (< 2000 g), lower doses and less frequent administration are recommended.
Maintenance dose (for 50 mg/kg/day divided q12h). In very low-birth weight neonates (< 2000 g), lower doses and less frequent administration are recommended.
Maintenance dose (for 50 mg/kg/day given once daily). In very low-birth weight neonates (< 2000 g), lower doses and less frequent administration are recommended.

indications: Broad-spectrum bacteriostatic antimicrobial, potentially bactericidal against H. influenzae and N. meningitidis.
Treatment of systemic anthrax (including meningitis or disseminated infection) in neonates ≥32 weeks gestation when preferred agents are unavailable.
Alternative agent for bacterial meningitis caused by susceptible strains of S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, and H. influenzae.

iv.instructions: Administer via intermittent infusion over 15 to 60 minutes. Adhere to NIOSH hazardous drug handling precautions, including the use of double gloves, protective gowns, and closed system drug transfer devices. Use eye and face protection if splashing is possible.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute the 1-g vial with 10 mL of sterile water for injection or D5W to reach a concentration of 100 mg/mL. Further dilute in a compatible solution to a final concentration of 20 to 25 mg/mL for infusion.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of vasomotor collapse and pallid cyanosis associated with 'gray baby' syndrome.
precautions.Hepatic: Metabolized via hepatic glucuronyl transferase; hydrolysis of the prodrug is erratic in newborns. Requires baseline and periodic hepatic function assessment.
precautions.Renal: Drug and metabolites are renally eliminated; requires baseline and periodic renal function assessment.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Potential for fungal overgrowth and abdominal distention (symptomatic of 'gray baby' syndrome).

serious: Gray baby syndrome (associated with serum levels >50 mcg/mL)
Fatal blood dyscrasias (hypoplastic anemia, thrombocytopenia, granulocytopenia)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Bacteriostatic agent acting as a prodrug (succinate and palmitate esters) that requires hydrolysis to the active compound; inhibits protein synthesis.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily eliminated via hepatic metabolism (glucuronyl transferase) and renal excretion.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Contains 52 mg (2.25 mEq) of sodium per gram. Therapeutic peak serum concentrations should be maintained between 10 to 25 mcg/mL.

admin_details.notes: Administer with food (improves absorption). Do not confuse with hydrochlorothiazide. IV administration not recommended.
Administer with food (improves absorption). Do not confuse with hydrochlorothiazide. IV administration not recommended.

indications: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD): Improves pulmonary function and decreases fractional oxygen requirements in ventilator-dependent infants.
Diabetes insipidus: Treatment of central and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.
Edema and Hypertension: Management of mild to moderate edema and hypertension; efficacy is enhanced when combined with furosemide or spironolactone.
Heart Failure: Supportive care for neonates exhibiting signs of right-sided heart failure associated with pulmonary hypertension.

oral.instructions: Typically administered in two divided doses. Often used in combination with spironolactone to improve pulmonary mechanics in preterm infants.
oral.preparation: Available as a 250 mg/5mL oral suspension.

iv.instructions: Use immediately after reconstitution. May be further diluted in compatible solutions (D5W or NS) for infusion.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute 500-mg vial with exactly 18 mL (never less) of sterile water for injection to achieve a concentration of 28 mg/mL.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Monitor blood pressure and clinical signs of right-sided heart failure.
precautions.Hepatic: Risk of toxicity in patients with significant hepatic impairment.
precautions.Renal: Elimination is GFR-dependent; contraindicated in significant renal failure.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Absorption from the GI tract is variable.

serious: Displacement of bilirubin from albumin binding sites (risk of kernicterus)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Inhibits sodium reabsorption in the distal nephron, leading to increased urinary excretion of sodium, potassium, magnesium, chloride, bicarbonate, and phosphorus, while decreasing renal calcium excretion.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal; dependent on Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted IV solution must be used immediately; discard any unused portion.

admin_details.notes: Intermittent IV infusion: Dilute in D5W or other compatible solution to a final concentration of 6 mg/mL.

ranges.notes: Administer every 6 to 12 hours.

admin_details.notes: Administer doses with meals and at bedtime. Do not administer simultaneously with antacids since antacids may interfere with cimetidine absorption.

ranges.notes: Administer every 6 to 12 hours.

indications: Prevention and treatment of stress ulcers and GI hemorrhage associated with gastric acid secretion
Second-line agent for Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) in infants and children when PPIs are unavailable or contraindicated
Treatment of erosive esophagitis (recommended duration 4 to 8 weeks)
Management of typical GERD symptoms in children as a diagnostic trial (4 to 8 weeks)

iv.instructions: Administer as an intermittent infusion over 15 to 20 minutes. Avoid rapid IV bolus administration to prevent cardiac arrhythmias and hypotension.
iv.preparation: Dilute in D5W or other compatible solution to a final concentration of 6 mg/mL. A 15 mg/mL dilution can be prepared by adding 1 mL of the 150 mg/mL concentrate to 9 mL of preservative-free normal saline.

oral.instructions: Administer doses with meals and at bedtime. Do not administer simultaneously with antacids as they interfere with absorption.
oral.preparation: Manufacturer's 60 mg/mL oral solution contains 2.8% alcohol.

reconstitution_table.method: Add 1 mL (60 mg) of manufacturer's oral solution to 24 mL of sterile water

contraindications: Concomitant use with cisapride due to risk of life-threatening arrhythmias

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of cardiac arrhythmias and hypotension following rapid IV bolus.
precautions.Hepatic: Half-life is prolonged in patients with hepatic insufficiency; drug is metabolized via liver sulfation and hydroxylation.
precautions.Renal: 90% of inactive compounds are renally eliminated; half-life is prolonged in patients with renal insufficiency.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Increased risk of necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC), especially in VLBW neonates, and Clostridium difficile infections.
precautions.Infection: Increased risk of pneumonia, sepsis (bacterial and fungal), and Candida species colonization in infants and children.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Inhibits gastric acid secretion through histamine H2-receptor antagonism.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: 90% renal elimination of inactive compounds

availability: Injectable solution: 150 mg/mL (2-mL single-use and 8-mL multidose vials)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: IV dilution (15 mg/mL) is stable for 48 hours. Oral dilution (2.4 mg/mL) is stable for 14 days when refrigerated.

admin_details.notes: Infuse not to exceed 30 mg/min. Standard concentration is 6 mg/mL. Discontinue if diarrhea or hypersensitivity occurs.

ranges.notes: FDA Dosage for PMA 32 weeks or less.
FDA Dosage for PMA 32 to 40 weeks.

admin_details.notes: Infuse not to exceed 30 mg/min. Standard concentration is 6 mg/mL.
Used as part of combination therapy for systemic anthrax. Duration depends on meningitis status (14 days to 60 days).
Used for naturally-acquired cutaneous infection or prophylaxis (up to 60 days).
Give with Pyrimethamine and Folinic acid. Add Prednisone if CSF protein > 1g/dL or severe chorioretinitis. Duration 3-12 months.
Give with Pyrimethamine and Folinic acid. Add Prednisone if CSF protein > 1g/dL or severe chorioretinitis. Duration 3-12 months.

indications: Treatment of bacteremia and pulmonary or deep tissue infections caused by anaerobic bacteria and gram-positive cocci.
Postexposure prophylaxis and treatment of Bacillus anthracis (Anthrax), including systemic and cutaneous involvement.
Alternative therapy for Toxoplasmosis in HIV-infected pediatric patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
Secondary prophylaxis of toxoplasmosis in HIV-infected patients.

iv.instructions: Infuse over 10 to 60 minutes. Maximum infusion rate is 30 mg/min. Concentration should not exceed 18 mg/mL for general use or 6 mg/mL for neonates. Do not administer as an undiluted bolus.
iv.preparation: Dilute with D5W or NS. Doses of 300-600 mg require 50 mL of diluent; doses of 900-1200 mg require 100 mL of diluent.

oral.instructions: Used for postexposure anthrax prophylaxis, cutaneous anthrax, or follow-up therapy for severe anthrax.
oral.preparation: Reconstitute clindamycin palmitate hydrochloride granules with water to achieve a final concentration of 75 mg/5 mL (15 mg/mL).

im.instructions: Route mentioned for pharmacokinetic profile; specific administration instructions not provided in text.

precautions.Hepatic: Severe hepatic disease requires monitoring of liver function tests.
precautions.Renal: Risk of acute kidney injury and renal failure; monitor renal function in patients with pre-existing dysfunction or those on nephrotoxic drugs.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: History of gastrointestinal disease, particularly colitis; high risk of Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD).
precautions.Immunologic: Risk of anaphylactic shock and severe skin reactions (SJS, TEN, DRESS); history of atopy increases risk.
precautions.Respiratory: Benzyl alcohol content in IV solutions can cause fatal 'gasping syndrome' in neonates/premature infants.

serious: Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and fatal colitis

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Bacteriostatic agent that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 23S RNA of the 50S subunit of the ribosome.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Extensive metabolism in the liver and intestine; major metabolite is clindamycin sulfoxide.

availability: Oral: 75 mg/5 mL granules for reconstitution
IV: 150 mg/mL solution in 2, 4, and 6 mL vials (contains benzyl alcohol)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted oral solution: stable for 2 weeks at room temperature (do not refrigerate). IV vials: discard 24 hours after entry. Diluted IV solution: stable at room temperature 25°C.

admin_details.notes: Experts recommend using a 5 mcg/mL dilution of the epidural formulation (diluted in NS) for oral use due to accuracy concerns with extemporaneous compounded oral suspensions.

ranges.duration: Discontinue based on NAS scores and patient stability.
ranges.notes: Initial loading dose of 0.5 to 1 mcg/kg required. Maintenance interval is every 4 to 6 hours. Dosing based on 35 weeks GA or older.

indications: Adjunct therapy for Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome (NAS) in neonates with intrauterine opioid exposure.
Monotherapy for Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome (NAS) in neonates ≥35 weeks gestational age.
Adjunctive sedation and analgesia for mechanically ventilated infants (limited evidence of efficacy).

oral.instructions: Use precise dilutions to ensure dosing accuracy in neonates; some experts recommend diluting epidural formulations to 5 mcg/mL for oral use.
oral.preparation: Extemporaneous suspensions (10–100 mcg/mL) can be prepared using tablets or powder mixed with vehicles such as Oral Mix, Ora-Blend, Inorpha, or simple syrup. Triturate tablets in a glass mortar and levigate with a small amount of vehicle before adding the remaining volume.

reconstitution_table.vehicle: Oral Mix or Oral Mix SF to 30 mL
Purified water paste + Simple Syrup NF to 60 mL

epidural.instructions: Strictly contraindicated for administration above the C4 dermatome. Monitor for apnea and respiratory depression when used as an anesthetic adjunct.
epidural.preparation: The 500 mcg/mL strength must be diluted with Normal Saline (NS) to a concentration not exceeding 100 mcg/mL. Use only preservative-free diluents.

contraindications: Epidural administration above the C4 dermatome

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of rebound hypertension, agitation, and tachycardia upon abrupt discontinuation; gradual tapering is required. Monitor heart rate and BP closely during and 48 hours after therapy.
precautions.Hepatic: Approximately 50% of the dose is metabolized in the liver.
precautions.Renal: Elimination half-life is significantly prolonged in patients with renal impairment.

common: Decrease in heart rate (7 to 10 beats per minute)
Slight increase in diastolic blood pressure (approx. 5 mm Hg) at higher doses
Rebound increase in systolic and diastolic blood pressure post-discontinuation
serious: Apnea (particularly when used with local anesthetics in neonates)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Centrally acting alpha-2-adrenergic agonist that stimulates brain stem receptors to decrease sympathetic outflow, reducing peripheral resistance, heart rate, and blood pressure.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Liver metabolism (50%); Renal excretion of unchanged drug (40% to 60%)

availability: 0.1 mg, 0.2 mg, 0.3 mg immediate-release oral tablets
100 mcg/mL and 500 mcg/mL preservative-free epidural injection vials
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Oral suspensions: 91 days at 25°C/4°C in Oral Mix; 60 days at 5°C in Inorpha; 35 days refrigerated in simple syrup (tablet-based); 28 days refrigerated for 100 mcg/mL concentration.

admin_details.notes: May be given without regard to feedings. Clopidogrel should be discontinued 5 days prior to elective surgery if an antiplatelet effect is not desired.

ranges.notes: Dose achieves similar antiplatelet effect (30% to 50% inhibition of ADP-induced platelet aggregation) as 75 mg/day in adults.

indications: Antiplatelet agent for the prophylaxis of thrombotic events.
Prophylaxis of thrombosis in cardiac disease and conditions associated with high arterial thrombosis risk.
Note: Not FDA approved for use in pediatric patients.

oral.instructions: May be administered without regard to meals. Nasogastric administration in critically ill patients post-cardiopulmonary resuscitation may result in impaired bioavailability.

contraindications: Active pathological bleeding (e.g., peptic ulcer or intracranial hemorrhage)

precautions.Cardiovascular: Premature discontinuation significantly increases the risk of stent thrombosis, myocardial infarction, and death; diminished antiplatelet effectiveness in CYP2C19 poor or intermediate metabolizers.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Increased risk of massive upper gastrointestinal bleeding when used concomitantly with aspirin and/or warfarin; avoid concurrent use with omeprazole or esomeprazole as they reduce platelet inhibition.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Clopidogrel is a prodrug that requires conversion to an active thiol metabolite, which selectively and irreversibly binds to the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets. This prevents activation of the GPIIb/IIIa complex, thereby inhibiting platelet aggregation.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Urine (50%) and feces (46%)

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Platelet inhibition is irreversible for the lifespan of the platelet (7–10 days); recovery of platelet function generally occurs 5 days after discontinuation.

admin_details.notes: Intermittent: Infuse over 3-5 min (concentration 75 mg/mL) or 30 min (if diluted in 5 mL NS). Continuous: Initial half-dose over 3-5 min, followed by remaining half over 22-23 hours.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose. Total daily dose 2.5 to 5 mg/kg/day divided every 6 to 12 hours. Maximum: 5 mg/kg/day. Continuous infusion requires an initial loading dose of one-half the total daily dose over 3-5 minutes.
Renal Adjustment: For CrCl 50 to 79 mL/min. Total daily dose 2.5 to 3.8 mg/kg/day.
Renal Adjustment: For CrCl 30 to 49 mL/min. Dose can be given once daily (2.5 mg/kg q24h) or divided into 2 doses per day (1.25 mg/kg q12h).
Renal Adjustment: For CrCl 10 to 29 mL/min.

admin_details.notes: Administer by deep IM injection into large muscle mass (e.g., gluteal muscles or lateral portion of thigh).

ranges.notes: Total daily dose 2.5 to 5 mg/kg/day divided every 6 to 12 hours.

indications: Treatment of multi-drug resistant gram-negative infections, specifically Acinetobacter baumannii and Klebsiella pneumoniae.
Treatment of ventilator-associated pneumonia in neonates (aerosolized administration).
Used as part of combination therapy with other IV antibiotics for severe sepsis and multi-organ dysfunction.

im.instructions: Administer via deep intramuscular injection into a large muscle mass, such as the gluteal muscles or the lateral portion of the thigh.
im.preparation: Reconstitute 150 mg vial with 2 mL sterile water for injection to achieve a concentration of 75 mg/mL colistin base.

iv.instructions: Continuous Infusion: Inject half the daily dose slowly over 3-5 minutes, then administer the remaining half via slow IV infusion over 22-23 hours. Intermittent Administration: Administer half the total daily dose slowly over 3-5 minutes every 12 hours. In neonates, doses may be added to 5 mL of normal saline and infused over 30 minutes.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute 150 mg vial with 2 mL sterile water for injection (75 mg/mL); swirl gently to avoid frothing. For continuous infusion, dilute further with compatible solutions.

inhalation.instructions: Administer via ultrasonic nebulizer over 15 minutes every 12 hours.
inhalation.preparation: Use immediately after mixing. Solutions older than 24 hours must be discarded due to the formation of toxic polymyxin E1 which causes airway inflammation.

precautions.Renal: Reversible, dose-dependent nephrotoxicity; increased risk of neuromuscular blockade and apnea in patients with renal impairment; dosage adjustments required based on renal function.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Risk of Clostridium difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) ranging from mild diarrhea to fatal colitis; discontinue therapy if suspected.
precautions.Respiratory: Risk of acute respiratory failure if inhalation solution is not used promptly; bronchospasm may occur (premedication with bronchodilators recommended); risk of respiratory arrest after IM administration.
precautions.Neurological: Transient disturbances including circumoral paresthesia, numbness, tingling of extremities, vertigo, dizziness, and slurred speech.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A bactericidal surface-active agent that penetrates and disrupts the cell membrane of aerobic gram-negative microorganisms.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primary route is renal (urine)

availability: Vials containing 150 mg colistin base activity (equivalent to 360 mg colistimethate sodium)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted solution (SWFI) is stable for 7 days at room temperature (20-25°C) or refrigeration (2-8°C). Continuous infusion mixtures must be used within 24 hours. Inhalation solutions should be used immediately and discarded after 24 hours.

admin_details.notes: Patients receiving corticosteroids such as cortisone or hydrocortisone, or patients receiving spironolactone, should omit pre-test doses of these drugs on test day.

ranges.notes: FDA dosage for patients younger than 2 years.
Alternative weight-based dosage for infants.
Low-dose (1 mcg) may be considered in the setting of short supply.

admin_details.notes: Patients receiving corticosteroids such as cortisone or hydrocortisone, or patients receiving spironolactone, should omit pre-test doses of these drugs on test day.

ranges.notes: FDA dosage for patients younger than 2 years.

indications: Diagnostic screening agent for adrenal cortical insufficiency in adult and pediatric patients.

iv.instructions: Administer via intravenous route. Monitor for hypersensitivity reactions during and after administration.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute 0.25 mg lyophilized powder with 1 mL of 0.9% sodium chloride for a final concentration of 0.25 mg/mL. May be further diluted in 0.9% sodium chloride (e.g., to concentrations of 0.5 mcg/mL or 5 mcg/mL).

im.instructions: Administer via intramuscular injection. For rapid screening, collect blood samples (6 to 7 mL) before injection and exactly 30 minutes post-injection. Refrigerate samples until lab assay.
im.preparation: Reconstitute 0.25 mg lyophilized powder with 1 mL of 0.9% sodium chloride for a final concentration of 0.25 mg/mL.

contraindications: History of hypersensitivity to cosyntropin or any of its excipients.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Synthetic subunit of natural ACTH that stimulates the adrenal gland to secrete 17-OH corticosteroids, 17-ketosteroids, and 17-ketogenic steroids; possesses extra-adrenal effects including melanotropic activity and increased growth hormone secretion.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted 0.25 mg/mL solution: Discard unused portions. Diluted solution (5 mcg/mL in NS): Approximately 90% potent at 2 months and 80% potent at 4 months when refrigerated (4°C). Diluted solution (0.5 mcg/mL in NS): Stable for up to 60 days when refrigerated (2 to 8°C).

admin_details.notes: Use solutions containing concentrations of 0.5% (5 mg/mL) or less in neonates. May be used in conjunction with 1 drop of phenylephrine 2.5% ophthalmic solution. Apply pressure to the lacrimal sac during and for 2 minutes after instillation to minimize systemic absorption.

ranges.notes: Instill drops 10 to 30 minutes prior to funduscopy.

indications: Induction of mydriasis for diagnostic and therapeutic ophthalmic procedures
Induction of cycloplegia for ophthalmic procedures

ophthalmic.instructions: Administer as topical eye drops. Perform lacrimal sac occlusion during administration to reduce systemic absorption via the nasolacrimal system. Withhold feedings for 4 hours following the procedure.
ophthalmic.preparation: A combination solution ('Caputo drops') can be prepared by mixing 3.75 mL of cyclopentolate 2%, 7.5 mL of tropicamide 1%, and 3.75 mL of phenylephrine 10% in a 15-mL bottle (Final concentration: cyclopentolate 0.5%, tropicamide 0.5%, phenylephrine 2.5%).

precautions.Cardiovascular: Monitor for tachycardia and vasodilatation due to systemic anticholinergic absorption.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Assess for signs of ileus prior to feeding; drug may cause delayed gastric emptying and decreased motility.

serious: Systemic toxicity (increased risk in infants using concentrations ≥ 1%)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Anticholinergic agent that inhibits the sphincter pupillae muscle and the ciliary muscle, resulting in pupillary dilation (mydriasis) and paralysis of accommodation (cycloplegia).

pharmacokinetics.bioavailability: Approximately 80% available for rapid systemic absorption via nasal mucosa if lacrimal sac is not occluded

availability: Cyclomydril: cyclopentolate 0.2% and phenylephrine 1% (2 and 5-mL Drop-tainers)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store away from heat; do not refrigerate.

admin_details.notes: Can be administered undiluted or can be diluted to a concentration of 0.1 to 1 mg/mL in NS for intravenous infusion. Administer as IV slow push.

ranges.notes: DART trial protocol: Step 1 of tapering schedule.
DART trial protocol: Step 2 of tapering schedule.
DART trial protocol: Step 3 of tapering schedule.
DART trial protocol: Step 4 of tapering schedule.

admin_details.notes: Take large doses with meals and take antacids between meals. Mix concentrate solution with liquid or semi-solid food and consume immediately. The IV formulation has been used orally in pediatric patients (e.g., mixed with juice).

ranges.notes: DART trial protocol: Step 1 of tapering schedule.
DART trial protocol: Step 2 of tapering schedule.
DART trial protocol: Step 3 of tapering schedule.
DART trial protocol: Step 4 of tapering schedule.

indications: Facilitation of extubation and acute lung function improvement in preterm infants at high risk for Chronic Lung Disease (CLD).
Adjunctive treatment for severe cerebral edema or meningoencephalitis associated with Anthrax.
Treatment of steroid-responsive inflammatory conditions of the eye (allergic conjunctivitis, keratitis, iritis, cyclitis).
Ophthalmic management of corneal injury from chemical, radiation, thermal burns, or foreign body penetration.
Systemic immunosuppression and management of various anti-inflammatory disorders.
Reduction of edema related to tumors, infection, or neurologic disorders.
Treatment of nephrotic syndrome in pediatric patients >2 years of age.
Palliative management of aggressive lymphomas and leukemias in infants >29 days of age.

iv.instructions: May be administered via undiluted slow injection or intermittent infusion.
iv.preparation: Dilute to a concentration of 0.1 to 1 mg/mL in Normal Saline (NS) for infusion.

ophthalmic.instructions: Shake suspension well before use; monitoring of intraocular pressure required for therapy >10 days.

oral.instructions: Administer large doses with meals; use antacids between meals to prevent peptic ulcers. IV formulation may be used orally if necessary.
oral.preparation: Mix concentrate solution with liquid or semi-solid food (water, juice, soda, applesauce, pudding) and consume immediately. Extemporaneous 0.5 mg/mL or 1 mg/mL suspensions can be prepared using Ora-Sweet/Ora-Plus or Oral Mix vehicles.

contraindications: Viral diseases of the cornea and conjunctiva (vaccinia, varicella)

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of hypertension, increased left ventricular wall thickness with outflow tract obstruction, and impaired left ventricular filling.
precautions.Renal: Increased risk of renal stones when used concurrently with furosemide.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Increased risk of gastrointestinal perforation and hemorrhage, particularly when initiated on Day 1 of life or used with indomethacin.

serious: Cerebral palsy (associated with early/high-dose use in neonates)
Spinal cord infarction or stroke (if administered epidurally)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Stabilizes lysosomal/cell membranes, inhibits prostaglandin and leukotriene production, reduces complement-induced granulocyte aggregation, and improves alveolar-capillary barrier integrity. Also increases surfactant production and suppresses pituitary ACTH secretion.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primary routes not specified; involves protein catabolism and increased urinary calcium excretion.

availability: Injection: 4 mg/mL, 10 mg/mL (preservative-free or benzyl alcohol)
Oral: 0.5 mg/5 mL solution, 1 mg/mL concentrate (Intensol), Tablets (0.5 mg to 6 mg)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: IV dilutions in NS stable 22–30 days depending on container; D5W dilutions stable 14 days. Extemporaneous oral suspensions stable up to 91 days. Intensol concentrate must be discarded 90 days after opening.

admin_details.notes: Generally, glucose concentrations greater than 15% should be administered via a central vein to minimize risk of phlebitis and thrombosis. Bolus doses should be administered only by slow IV injection. Abruptly discontinuing a dextrose infusion is not recommended due the risk for rebound hypoglycemia.

ranges.notes: Initial maintenance glucose infusion rate. Initial loading dose: 200 mg/kg (2 mL/kg) as D10W. Titrate rate to attain normoglycemia. Higher doses (10 to 20 mg/kg/minute) may be necessary for persistent hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia.

admin_details.notes: Prior to application, dry the mouth with a gauze. Massage the gel into the buccal mucosa followed by breastfeeding.

ranges.notes: Dextrose gel (40%). May be repeated up to 6 doses over 48 hours.

admin_details.notes: Initial continuous infusion. Dosages are adjusted based on serum glucose and potassium concentrations.

ranges.notes: Administer with 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg/hour regular insulin.

admin_details.notes: Advanced as tolerated to a goal rate.

ranges.notes: Initial rate for preterm neonates.
Initial rate for neonates. Advanced as tolerated to a goal rate of 10 to 12 mg/kg/minute.

indications: Treatment of hyperkalemia (in combination with insulin)
Prophylaxis of neonatal hypoglycemia in at-risk infants
Treatment of hypoglycemia in late preterm and term neonates
Management of hypoglycemia during cardiopulmonary resuscitation
Caloric nutritional supplement in parenteral nutrition solutions

iv.instructions: Administer concentrations >15% via a central vein to minimize phlebitis and thrombosis risk. Administer bolus doses only by slow IV injection. Avoid abrupt discontinuation to prevent rebound hypoglycemia.
iv.preparation: For hypoglycemia, maintain continuous infusion using 5% to 10% dextrose solutions with appropriate maintenance electrolytes.

buccal.instructions: Dry the oral mucosa with gauze prior to application. Massage the gel into the buccal mucosa; in neonates, follow administration with breastfeeding.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Hypertonic solutions (25% and 50%) increase risk of injection site phlebitis and thrombosis; rapid administration may lead to hyperosmolar syndrome.
precautions.Hepatic: Excessive glucose intake via parenteral nutrition is associated with liver impairment and steatosis.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Restores blood glucose levels and provides carbohydrate calories (3.4 kcal/g). In hyperkalemia treatment, dextrose works with insulin to stimulate the sodium-potassium adenosine triphosphatase (Na-K ATPase) pump, facilitating an intracellular shift of potassium.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Summarized storage times not provided in text; consult specific product labeling for stability post-dilution.

admin_details.notes: Shake suspension well before administration.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose: 8 to 15 mg/kg/day orally in equally divided doses every 8 to 12 hours. Initial Dose: 10 mg/kg/day orally divided every 8 hours (approx 3.33 mg/kg/dose). If not effective within 2 to 3 weeks, then discontinue. Renal Impairment: No specific neonatal recommendations, but a reduced dosage should be considered.

indications: Treatment of hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia in small-for-gestational-age infants.
Management of hypoglycemia associated with leucine sensitivity, islet cell hyperplasia, nesidioblastosis, extrapancreatic malignancy, islet cell adenoma, or adenomatosis.
Preoperative and postoperative stabilization for patients with persistent hypoglycemia.

oral.instructions: Shake oral suspension well before each use. Monitor blood glucose closely during initiation and stabilization. Dosage adjustments may be required when transitioning between oral capsules and oral suspension due to differences in bioavailability.
oral.preparation: Extemporaneous 10 mg/mL suspension: Can be prepared using bulk powder (mixed with glycerine then Oral Mix/SF) or by pulverizing 100 mg capsules and mixing with Oral Mix/SF to the required volume.

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to diazoxide or other thiazide derivatives

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of fluid retention leading to congestive heart failure, particularly in patients with compromised cardiac reserve; antihypertensive effects may be potentiated if coadministered with other antihypertensives.
precautions.Hepatic: Risk of displacing bilirubin from albumin in newborns with bilirubinemia.
precautions.Renal: Increased risk of drug toxicity in patients with impaired renal function; monitoring of serum electrolytes, BUN, and creatinine clearance is required.
precautions.Other_Precautions: Risk of pulmonary hypertension in infants; potential for ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar coma during illness; long-term use (4+ years) associated with abnormal facial features; may cause hyperuricemia.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A nondiuretic benzothiadiazine derivative that induces rapid elevation of blood glucose by inhibiting pancreatic insulin release and exerting extrapancreatic effects. It also decreases sodium and water excretion.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primary renal excretion

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Extemporaneous 10 mg/mL suspension is stable for 90 days at 5°C or 25°C in amber plastic oral syringes or PET bottles. Protect commercial products from light and store at room temperature.

admin_details.notes: Preferably, administer on an empty stomach (30 minutes before or 2 hours after a feeding). Shake well before measuring dose.

ranges.notes: Manufacturer dose.
Guideline dose; may be more appropriate for neonates.

indications: Treatment of HIV-1 infection in pediatric patients aged 2 weeks and older, in combination with other antiretroviral agents.
Preferred initial regimen for infants 14 days or older (and ≥42 weeks post-gestational age) typically includes 2 NRTIs plus lopinavir/ritonavir-boosted therapy.

oral.instructions: Administer preferably on an empty stomach (30 minutes before or 2 hours after a feeding). Shake the container well before measuring each dose.
oral.preparation: Reconstitute the 2 g or 4 g bottle with 100 mL or 200 mL of purified water, respectively, to achieve an initial concentration of 20 mg/mL. Immediately mix this solution with one part Maximum Strength Mylanta Liquid to reach a final concentration of 10 mg/mL.

precautions.Hepatic: Severe hepatomegaly with steatosis and non-cirrhotic portal hypertension have been reported, including fatalities; monitor for signs like thrombocytopenia or splenomegaly and discontinue if hepatotoxicity is suspected.
precautions.Renal: Approximately 50% of the drug is eliminated renally; monitoring is required.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Fatal and nonfatal pancreatitis may occur; suspend treatment for suspected pancreatitis and discontinue for confirmed cases.
precautions.Endocrine_Metabolic: Risk of lipoatrophy (related to cumulative exposure), lactic acidosis (especially in pregnancy with stavudine), insulin resistance, and diabetes mellitus.
precautions.Immunologic: Potential for immune reconstitution syndrome and autoimmune disorders (e.g., Graves' disease, polymyositis) occurring months after treatment start.
precautions.Neurologic: Peripheral neuropathy risk, particularly in patients with advanced HIV or concurrent neurotoxic therapy.
precautions.Ophthalmic: Retinal changes and optic neuritis reported; periodic retinal examinations are recommended.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) that inhibits the replication of HIV type 1.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primary renal excretion (50%)

availability: 2 g pediatric powder for oral solution (4-ounce bottle)
4 g pediatric powder for oral solution (8-ounce bottle)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted admixture with Mylanta is stable for up to 30 days when stored under refrigeration at 2 to 8 degrees C (36 to 46 degrees F).

admin_details.notes: Reconstitute 40 mg vial with 4 mL Sterile Water for 10 mg/mL. May further dilute with 36 mL NS to 1 mg/mL. Administer by slow IV infusion; if cardiac arrest is imminent, can be given by bolus injection. Stop temporarily for infusion-rate related anaphylactoid reactions.

ranges.notes: Chronic toxicity with unknown digoxin level: single vial (40 mg) IV initially. Readministration may be required if toxicity has not reversed after several hours.
Chronic toxicity with known digoxin level: Dose (mg) = 40 * (serum digoxin concentration in ng/mL * weight in kg) / 100.

Acute Ingestion: Dose (vials) = (digoxin ingested (mg) * bioavailability) / 0.5. Bioavailability: solution = 0.85, tablets = 0.8. Use the higher dose estimate if results differ from serum-based calculation.

indications: Treatment of life-threatening toxicity or overdose from digoxin or digitoxin.
Management of poisoning from cardiac glycoside-containing plants (e.g., foxglove, yellow oleander).
Treatment of Bufo toad venom ingestion.
Pediatric use for acute ingestion of ≥4 mg (or 0.1 mg/kg) of digoxin.
Pediatric use for serum digoxin concentrations >10 ng/mL (acute) or >4 ng/mL (chronic).
Management of severe manifestations of digitalis toxicity, including ventricular arrhythmias, progressive bradycardia, high-degree heart block, and hyperkalemia (>6 mEq/L).

iv.instructions: Administer via slow IV infusion over 30 minutes. If cardiac arrest is imminent, the dose may be administered by bolus injection. Monitor for infusion-rate related anaphylactoid reactions (hypotension, wheezing, urticaria); if these occur, temporarily stop the infusion and restart at a lower rate.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute each 40 mg vial with 4 mL of Sterile Water for Injection to achieve a 10 mg/mL concentration. Mix gently. For administration, further dilute with Normal Saline (NS). For very small doses (<1 mL), use a tuberculin syringe to deliver undiluted reconstituted solution, or further dilute the 10 mg/mL solution with 36 mL of NS to reach a 1 mg/mL concentration.

contraindications: Previous allergic reaction to intact ovine antibodies or ovine Fab fragments

precautions.Cardiovascular: Potential for clinical deterioration in patients with poor cardiac function due to the rapid loss of digoxin's inotropic effect; risk of worsening CHF and atrial fibrillation.
precautions.Renal: Elimination half-life may increase up to 10-fold in renal impairment; monitoring of free digoxin levels is recommended to detect possible recurrence of toxicity.
precautions.Electrolytes: Rapid reversal of digitalis effect may cause shift of potassium into cells, resulting in hypokalemia; frequent monitoring required.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Ovine-derived monovalent Fab immunoglobulin fragments bind to serum digoxin or digitoxin molecules. This binding facilitates the shift of tissue-bound digoxin into the extracellular fluid to maintain equilibrium, where it is further bound by the Fab fragments and subsequently excreted renally.

pharmacokinetics.half_life: 15 hours (normal renal function); up to 150 hours (renal impairment)
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Excreted in the urine as digoxin-Fab complexes; poorly removed by hemodialysis

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store unreconstituted vials in the refrigerator (do not freeze). Reconstituted solution should be used immediately. If not used immediately, it must be refrigerated and used within 4 hours.

admin_details.notes: Do not administer IM. Titrate dosage based on clinical response. Decrease dose proportional to the reduction in creatinine clearance.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose ('Digitalization') of 15 mcg/kg IV required, divided into 3 doses over 24 hours.
Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose ('Digitalization') of 20 mcg/kg IV required, divided into 3 doses over 24 hours.
Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose ('Digitalization') of 30 mcg/kg IV required, divided into 3 doses over 24 hours.
Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose ('Digitalization') of 40 mcg/kg IV required, divided into 3 doses over 24 hours.

admin_details.notes: Give consistently with regard to feedings. Oral doses should be 25% greater than IV doses. Titrate dosage based on clinical response. Decrease dose proportional to the reduction in creatinine clearance.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose ('Digitalization') of 20 mcg/kg Oral required, divided into 3 doses over 24 hours.
Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose ('Digitalization') of 25 mcg/kg Oral required, divided into 3 doses over 24 hours.
Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose ('Digitalization') of 40 mcg/kg Oral required, divided into 3 doses over 24 hours.
Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose ('Digitalization') of 50 mcg/kg Oral required, divided into 3 doses over 24 hours.

indications: Treatment of heart failure caused by diminished myocardial contractility
Management of supraventricular tachycardia (SVT), atrial flutter, and atrial fibrillation
Supportive care for right-sided heart failure in neonates with pulmonary hypertension (limited data)
Note: Rarely used in pediatric populations; not for acute deterioration

iv.instructions: Infuse concentrations of 20 mcg/mL or 100 mcg/mL over a period of 15 to 30 minutes.
iv.preparation: Draw digoxin into a syringe, then inject into a second syringe containing a 4-fold or greater volume of compatible diluent. Must be used immediately after dilution.

oral.instructions: Administer consistently in relation to feedings to maintain stable absorption.

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to digoxin or other digitalis preparations

precautions.Cardiovascular: High risk of ventricular fibrillation in WPW syndrome with atrial fibrillation; potential for severe sinus bradycardia or SA block in patients with preexisting node disease; risk of ventricular arrhythmias during electrical cardioversion; not recommended for myocarditis or acute myocardial infarction.
precautions.Renal: Renal impairment increases risk of toxicity; exposure is increased by drugs affecting renal function (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, NSAIDs, COX-2 inhibitors).
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Decreased cardiac output may occur in patients with heart failure and preserved left ventricular systolic function.

common: ECG changes (QTc shortening, ST segment sagging, T-wave dampening)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A digitalis glycoside providing positive inotropic and negative chronotropic effects. Inhibits sarcolemmal Na-K-ATPase to increase intracellular calcium and contractility; increases vagal activity to slow SA and AV node conduction.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily cleared via glomerular filtration and tubular secretion; significant nonrenal elimination also proposed.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store at room temperature and protect from light; diluted products must be used immediately.

admin_details.notes: Avoid bolus administration. Infusion into a large vein is preferred to minimize risk of tissue extravasation. Solutions may exhibit a pink color due to oxidation; there is no significant loss of potency over 24 hours.

ranges.notes: Continuous IV infusion. Begin at a low dose and titrate by monitoring effects. May also administer via IO route.

indications: Treatment of hypoperfusion and hypotension, particularly when related to myocardial dysfunction.
Hemodynamic support for severe sepsis and septic shock.
Management of fluid-refractory shock in pediatric and neonatal populations.

iv.instructions: Administer via continuous infusion only; avoid bolus administration. Use of a large vein is preferred to minimize the risk of tissue extravasation. Continuous heart rate and intra-arterial blood pressure monitoring are recommended.
iv.preparation: Dilute vials in a compatible diluent to a maximum concentration of 4000 mcg/mL (2000 mcg/mL is recommended for neonates). To calculate dose: Desired concentration (mg/mL) x final volume (mL) = total mg required.

io.instructions: May be administered via the intraosseous route as a continuous infusion for emergency hemodynamic support.
io.preparation: Dilute in compatible diluent up to a concentration of 4000 mcg/mL.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of dysrhythmias, increased heart rate, and blood pressure fluctuations. May increase pulmonary capillary wedge pressure leading to congestion and edema.
precautions.Hepatic: Primary site of metabolism; wide interpatient variability in plasma clearance due to metabolic differences.
precautions.Renal: Clearance variability exists due to differences in renal excretion of metabolites.
precautions.Dermatologic: Risk of phlebitis and local inflammatory changes or tissue damage if infiltration/extravasation occurs.

common: Local inflammatory changes at injection site

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Synthetic catecholamine with primary β1-adrenergic activity. Acts as an inotropic vasopressor to increase myocardial contractility, cardiac index, and oxygen delivery. It typically decreases systemic and pulmonary vascular resistance.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Hepatic metabolism via sulfoconjugation to inactive compounds; excreted renally.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Diluted solutions are stable for 24 hours. Oxidation may cause a pink discoloration over time, but this does not indicate a significant loss of potency. Avoid co-infusion with alkaline solutions (like fat emulsions pH 6-9) as the drug may degrade.

admin_details.notes: Continuous IV infusion. Vials must be diluted prior to use in compatible diluent up to a concentration of 3.2 mg/mL. The recommended standard neonate concentration is 1.6 mg/mL. Infusion into a large vein is preferred to minimize risk of tissue extravasation. Avoid bolus administration.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose. Begin at a low dose and titrate by monitoring effects; for fluid refractory shock start at less than 10 mcg/kg/min.

indications: Management of hypotension
Treatment of severe sepsis and septic shock
Hemodynamic support for fluid-refractory shock (Cold and Warm shock)
First-line management for neonatal hemodynamic instability

iv.instructions: Administer via continuous infusion; avoid bolus administration. Infusion into a large vein is preferred to minimize the risk of tissue extravasation. Monitor heart rate and intra-arterial blood pressure continuously. Assess urine output and peripheral perfusion frequently.
iv.preparation: Dilute vials prior to use in compatible diluent up to a maximum concentration of 3200 mcg/mL. Standard neonatal concentration is 1600 mcg/mL. Do not use if the solution exhibits a color change. Avoid co-infusion with alkaline solutions (pH 6-9), such as fat emulsions, as degradation may occur.

io.instructions: Administer via continuous infusion; avoid bolus administration. Monitor site closely for infiltration.
io.preparation: Dilute to compatible concentrations (up to 3200 mcg/mL) using standard calculations: Desired final concentration (mg/mL) × final volume (mL) = mg of drug required.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of tachycardia and arrhythmias. May increase pulmonary artery pressure; use higher doses with caution in patients with persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN).
precautions.Renal: Low doses (2 to 5 mcg/kg/min) may cause selective renal vasodilation and increased urine output in preterm neonates.

serious: Tissue sloughing and necrosis following extravasation (Black Box Warning)
Severe tissue damage requiring phentolamine infiltration

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Catecholamine that increases blood pressure primarily by increasing systemic vascular resistance via α-adrenergic effects; stimulates renal vasodilation at low doses.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Rapidly metabolized

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Diluted solutions are stable for 24 hours. Drug is most stable at pH ≤ 5; potential degradation in alkaline environments (pH 6-9). Admixtures showing color change must be discarded.

admin_details.notes: Administer via nebulizer. Standard solution is 1 mg/mL.

ranges.notes: Administer once or twice per day (every 12 to 24 hours).

admin_details.notes: Instill directly into the endotracheal tube.

ranges.notes: Administer once or twice per day (every 12 to 24 hours). Dose based on 1 mg/mL concentration (0.2 mL/kg).

indications: Treatment of atelectasis secondary to mucus plugging that has not responded to conventional therapies.

precautions.Respiratory: Monitor airway patency closely; rapid mobilization of secretions may require active suctioning.

serious: Airway obstruction secondary to rapid mobilization of secretions

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A recombinant human deoxyribonuclease (rhDNase) that selectively cleaves extracellular DNA released by degenerating leukocytes in purulent pulmonary secretions, significantly reducing mucus viscoelasticity.

pharmacokinetics.onset_of_action: Clinical improvement in secretion thickness and ventilation typically occurs within 3 hours.

admin_details.notes: Administer with adequate amounts of fluid to minimize esophageal irritation/ulceration. Administer with food or milk if gastric irritation occurs. Postmenstrual age range: 32 to 44 weeks.

ranges.notes: Duration: 60 days for aerosol exposure; 7 days for nonaerosol exposure. Cutaneous: 7 to 10 days. Systemic: At least 2 weeks.
Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose of 5 mg/kg required. Duration: 60 days for aerosol exposure; 7 days for nonaerosol exposure. Cutaneous: 7 to 10 days. Systemic: At least 2 weeks.

admin_details.notes: Avoid rapid administration. pH 1.8 to 3.3. Extravasation: acidic agent risk; stop infusion, aspirate, apply dry heat/elevation. Hyaluronidase 15 units intradermally for refractory events. Renal/Hepatic impairment: No adjustment necessary.

ranges.notes: Note: Dose is fixed 5 mg as per text. Switch to oral therapy based on clinical improvement.
Maintenance Dose. Initial loading dose of 5 mg/kg IV required. Switch to oral therapy based on clinical improvement.

indications: Empiric and preferred treatment for Anthrax (Postexposure prophylaxis, Cutaneous, and Systemic with/without meningitis)
Treatment of Rocky Mountain spotted fever in pediatric patients where benefits outweigh risks

oral.instructions: Administer with adequate fluids to minimize esophageal irritation and ulceration; may be taken with food or milk if gastric irritation occurs.
oral.preparation: For emergency extemporaneous use: Soak one 100-mg tablet in 20 mL water for 10 minutes, crush until no visible pieces remain, and mix the required dose with 3 teaspoons of milk, formula, or sweetened apple juice.

iv.instructions: Avoid rapid administration. In case of extravasation (pH 1.8–3.3), stop infusion, aspirate gently, apply dry heat, and elevate the site. Monitor for ischemia. Refractory cases may require hyaluronidase (15 units) or surgical intervention.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute 100-mg vial with 10 mL or 200-mg vial with 20 mL SWFI (10 mg/mL). Further dilute to a final concentration of 0.1 mg/mL to 1 mg/mL using NS, D5W, or LR.

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to any tetracycline class antibiotic

precautions.Hepatic: Periodic evaluation of hepatic function is required during long-term therapy.
precautions.Renal: May cause dose-related increases in BUN; periodic renal studies required during long-term therapy.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Risk of Clostridium difficile-associated diarrhea (mild to fatal) up to 2 months post-treatment; risk of permanent tooth discoloration or enamel hypoplasia in children under 8 years.
precautions.Dermatologic: Risk of severe reactions including SJS, TEN, DRESS, and photosensitivity; discontinue at first sign of erythema.
precautions.Neurologic: Risk of intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri) which may cause permanent vision loss; bulging fontanels reported in infants.
precautions.Musculoskeletal: Decreased skeletal growth reported in premature infants; typically reversible upon discontinuation.
precautions.Other: Avoid concomitant use with Isotretinoin; risk of fungal or bacterial superinfection.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 30S ribosomal subunit; exhibits bacteriostatic activity against various Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primary routes not specified; monitored via renal and hepatic studies.

availability: Oral: Powder for suspension (25 mg/5 mL), syrup (50 mg/5 mL), tablets/capsules (40 mg to 200 mg)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: IV: Stable for 48 hours at 25°C in NS/D5W under fluorescent light; 72 hours if refrigerated and protected from light (infusion must then not exceed 12 hours). Oral extemporaneous mixture: Stable for 24 hours at 20-25°C.

admin_details.notes: Daily requirements from ASPEN. Assumes normal age-related organ function and normal losses.

ranges.notes: Preterm neonates daily requirement.
Infants/children daily requirement.
