ranges.notes: Maintenance dose. Adjust to achieve anti-factor Xa level of 0.35-0.7 (aPTT 60-85s) 4 hours after initiation. Renal vein thrombosis may require 6 weeks to 3 months. May switch to LMWH after 3-5 days.

indications: Anticoagulation during Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (ECMO)
Maintenance of peripheral arterial and central venous catheter patency via continuous infusion
Treatment of unilateral or bilateral renal vein thrombosis

iv.instructions: Administer loading doses over 10 minutes. Maintenance therapy should be delivered via continuous infusion to maintain target activated clotting time (ACT) or aPTT. Ensure correct concentration is selected to avoid fatal medication errors.
iv.preparation: Dilute in compatible solutions to concentrations typically ranging from 10 to 500 units/mL for continuous infusion; 100 to 500 units/mL for loading doses.

im.instructions: Strictly avoid intramuscular administration due to the high risk of hematoma formation.

contraindications: Infants with evidence of intracranial bleeding

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of aortic thrombosis associated with Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT).
precautions.Hepatic: Metabolized by the liver; clearance varies by age.
precautions.Renal: Excreted renally; clearance may be delayed in some patients.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: High risk of bleeding; contraindicated in active GI hemorrhage.
precautions.Other: Long-term therapeutic use may lead to osteoporosis. Be aware of a 10% reduction in potency in USP-standardized products (marked with 'N').

serious: Fatal hemorrhage (often due to concentration errors)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Activates antithrombin III, which inactivates thrombin and factor Xa, preventing the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Liver metabolism; renal excretion (typically within 6 hours).

availability: Premixed bags (D5W, NS, ½ NS) in various concentrations
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Keep protamine sulfate available as an antidote for hemorrhage.

admin_details.notes: Recommended site: anterolateral thigh. Use 22- to 25-gauge needle (7/8 to 1 inch). Draw back on plunger to ensure needle is not in a blood vessel. When given with HepB vaccine, use separate syringe and different site.

ranges.notes: HBsAg-positive mother, any birth weight: Administer within 12 hours of birth. If HepB vaccination is delayed by 3 months, give a second 0.5-mL dose at 3 months. If vaccination is refused, give second dose at 3 months and third dose at 6 months.
HBsAg-unknown mother, birth weight less than 2000 g: Administer within 12 hours of birth.
HBsAg-unknown mother, birth weight 2000 g or greater: Administer only if mother is found to be positive; give as soon as possible but no later than 7 days of age.

indications: Passive immunization of newborns born to mothers who are HBsAg-positive.
Passive immunization of newborns whose mothers have acute hepatitis B infection at the time of delivery.

im.instructions: Administer in the anterolateral thigh for infants. Use a 22- to 25-gauge needle, 7/8 inch to 1 inch in length. If co-administered with the hepatitis B vaccine, use a separate syringe and a different injection site. Aspirate prior to injection to avoid intravascular delivery. Do not administer intravenously.

contraindications: Intravenous administration (risk of serious systemic reactions)

precautions.General: Universal precautions should be observed with neonates until they have been bathed to remove maternal blood and secretions.

common: Local pain at the injection site
serious: Serious systemic reactions (associated with IV administration)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Provides passive immunity via a hyperimmune globulin solution containing high titers of hepatitis B surface antigen antibodies (anti-HBsAg) derived from pooled human plasma.

availability: Nabi-HB and HepaGam: 1-mL or 5-mL single-dose vials
HyperHEP B: 0.5-mL neonatal single-dose syringe, 1-mL single-dose syringe, 1-mL single-dose vial, 5-mL single-dose vial
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store refrigerated at 2 to 8°C (36 to 46°F). Nabi-HB and HepaGam must be used within 6 hours after the vial has been entered.

admin_details.notes: Use a 22- to 25-gauge needle. The appropriate needle length is 7/8” to 1” for infants. Engerix-B concentration is 10 mcg/0.5 mL; Recombivax HB concentration is 5 mcg/0.5 mL.

ranges.notes: Engerix-B brand (10 mcg). Maternal HBsAg-Positive or Unknown: Administer first dose before 12 hours of age regardless of birth weight. If HBsAg positive, give with 0.5 mL HBIG. If HBsAg unknown and < 2000 g, give with 0.5 mL HBIG.

Recombivax HB brand (5 mcg). Maternal HBsAg-Positive or Unknown: Administer first dose before 12 hours of age regardless of birth weight. If HBsAg positive, give with 0.5 mL HBIG. If HBsAg unknown and < 2000 g, give with 0.5 mL HBIG.
Engerix-B brand (10 mcg). Maternal HBsAg-Negative: Administer first dose within 24 hours of birth for medically stable infants >= 2000 g.
Recombivax HB brand (5 mcg). Maternal HBsAg-Negative: Administer first dose within 24 hours of birth for medically stable infants >= 2000 g.
Engerix-B brand (10 mcg). Maternal HBsAg-Negative: For infants < 2000 g, administer 1 dose at chronological age 1 month or hospital discharge (whichever is earlier).
Recombivax HB brand (5 mcg). Maternal HBsAg-Negative: For infants < 2000 g, administer 1 dose at chronological age 1 month or hospital discharge (whichever is earlier).

admin_details.notes: May be administered subcutaneously in patients at risk for hemorrhage following IM injection, but the immune response may be lower.

ranges.notes: Engerix-B (10 mcg). Use only if IM is contraindicated due to hemorrhage risk.
Recombivax HB (5 mcg). Use only if IM is contraindicated due to hemorrhage risk.

indications: Immunoprophylaxis against hepatitis B infection
Vaccination for premature infants regardless of gestational age or birthweight
Safe for administration in infants born to HIV-positive mothers

im.instructions: Preferred route of administration. Use a 22- to 25-gauge needle. For infants, the appropriate needle length is 7/8” to 1”. Shake well before withdrawal and use.
im.preparation: Supplied as a preservative-free suspension; use as supplied and do not dilute.

subcutaneous.instructions: Indicated for patients at risk for hemorrhage following intramuscular injection. Note that the resulting immune response may be lower than the IM route.
subcutaneous.preparation: Supplied as a preservative-free suspension; use as supplied and do not dilute.

contraindications: Serious allergic reaction (e.g., anaphylaxis) after a previous hepatitis B vaccine dose
Hypersensitivity to any component of the vaccine, including yeast

precautions.General: Defer vaccination in patients with moderate or severe acute illness; caution in latex-sensitive individuals as Engerix-B® tip caps may contain natural rubber latex; immunocompromised patients may show suboptimal response.
precautions.Neurological: Syncope and associated neurological signs (tonic-clonic movements, paresthesias) may occur following injection.
precautions.Respiratory: Potential for apnea following IM administration in some premature infants.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Inactivated recombinant vaccine produced by genetically modified Saccharomyces cerevisiae; contains HBsAg protein adsorbed to aluminum hydroxide to induce active immunity.

availability: Recombivax HB®: 0.5-mL single-dose vials and prefilled syringes (5 mcg)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store refrigerated at 2 to 8 degrees C (36 to 46 degrees F). Do not freeze, as freezing destroys vaccine potency.

admin_details.notes: Use a separate syringe for each vaccine and give at different sites. Draw back on the plunger of the syringe before injection to be certain the needle is not in a blood vessel.

ranges.notes: Administer in the anterolateral thigh. Premature infants should be immunized according to their postnatal age; however, consider delaying the first dose in chronically ill premature infants due to inadequate seroconversion. Refer to most recent AAP/ACIP immunization schedule.

indications: Vaccination against invasive disease caused by Haemophilus influenzae type b in infants (6 weeks to 15 months of age).
Vaccination against infection caused by all known subtypes of hepatitis B virus in infants (6 weeks to 15 months of age) born to HBsAg-negative mothers.

injection.instructions: Administer using a separate syringe for each vaccine given. Rotate injection sites. Perform aspiration (draw back on the plunger) before injection to ensure the needle is not positioned within a blood vessel.
injection.preparation: Ensure the vaccine is a preservative-free liquid supplied in a single-dose vial.

contraindications: Serious allergic reaction (e.g., anaphylaxis) following a previous dose or to any vaccine component.
Infants younger than 6 weeks of age (due to the Hib component).

precautions.General: Defer vaccination in patients experiencing moderate or severe acute illness, with or without fever. Immunocompromised patients may exhibit a suboptimal immune response to the vaccine.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Combines antigenic components of Recombivax HB and PedvaxHIB to induce protective antibody formation (anti-HBs). The PRP-conjugate forms a T-dependent antigen that stimulates enhanced antibody response and long-term immunologic memory.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store under refrigeration. Do not freeze.

admin_details.notes: Administer before administration of other drugs or add to the injected solution of other injected or subQ infused drugs.

ranges.notes: Standard dose is 150 units.

admin_details.notes: Initiate treatment within 1 to 2 hours of extravasation; however, benefit may be seen up to 10 days later. Use 25- or 26-gauge needle and change after each injection. Elevate extremity and place saline-soaked gauze over the area.

ranges.notes: Undiluted method: Inject 1 mL (150 units) as 5 separate 0.2-mL subQ injections around the periphery of the site.
Diluted method: Dilute 0.1 mL (15 units) with 0.9 mL Normal Saline to give 15 units/mL. Inject 1 mL total as 5 separate 0.2-mL injections around the periphery.

admin_details.notes: Anatomical areas include mid-anterior thigh or interscapular area. Rate and volume of rehydration fluids should not exceed appropriate IV rates. Max volume 25 mL/kg at no faster than 2 mL/min.

ranges.notes: Administer prior to infusion of fluids. Hyaluronidase may be added to small volumes of solutions (up to 200 mL).

admin_details.notes: Followed by injection of the contrast medium at the same site.

ranges.notes: Inject 75 units over each scapula (Total 150 units).

indications: Prevention of tissue injury following IV extravasation of hyperosmolar or extreme pH medications (e.g., vancomycin, TPN, calcium, phenytoin).
Adjuvant in subcutaneous fluid administration (hypodermoclysis) to achieve hydration.
Adjuvant to enhance the dispersion and absorption of other injected medications.
Adjunct in subcutaneous urography to improve the resorption of radiopaque agents.

subcutaneous.instructions: For hydration (hypodermoclysis): Rate and volume should not exceed IV standards. For infants, max volume is 25 mL/kg at a rate no faster than 2 mL/min.
subcutaneous.preparation: May be added to small volumes of solutions (up to 200 mL) or directly to subcutaneous drug solutions.

infiltration.instructions: For extravasation: Initiate treatment within 1 to 2 hours of incident (effective up to 10 days). Elevate extremity and apply saline-soaked gauze. Use hydrogel/hydrocolloid dressings.
infiltration.preparation: Use at concentrations of 15 units/mL or undiluted at 150 units/mL.

intramuscular.instructions: Administer as an adjuvant to increase dispersion and absorption of other injectable agents.

interstitial.instructions: Approved for interstitial use to facilitate fluid or drug dispersion.

intraocular_retrobulbar_peribulbar.instructions: Approved for ophthalmic use including intraocular, retrobulbar, and peribulbar administration.

soft_tissue.instructions: Indicated for soft tissue injection to facilitate absorption.

contraindications: Do not inject near or into infected or acutely inflamed areas to avoid spreading infection.
Do not use to enhance absorption of dopamine or alpha-agonist vasoconstrictive agents.
Known hypersensitivity to hyaluronidase or any component of the formulation.

precautions.General: Perform a skin test (0.02 mL intradermally of 150-200 units/mL) to rule out hypersensitivity. Discontinue if sensitization occurs. Recombinant products (Hylenex) contain albumin, carrying a remote risk of viral or Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease transmission.

serious: Potential spread of localized infection if injected into inflamed areas

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A mucolytic enzyme that temporarily disrupts the normal intercellular barrier by hydrolyzing hyaluronic acid, facilitating the rapid dispersion and absorption of extravasated fluids or injected drugs through tissues.

availability: Hylenex (Human Recombinant): 150 USP units/mL single-use vial
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store refrigerated; do not freeze. Vitrase must be protected from light. Hydase and Hylenex contain edetate disodium.

admin_details.notes: Administer undiluted (20 mg/mL) or further dilution (e.g., 1 mg/mL) may be necessary.

ranges.notes: Begin with 0.1 to 0.5 mg/kg/dose every 6 to 8 hours. Gradually increase as required for BP control to a maximum of 2 mg/kg/dose every 6 hours. Use with a beta-blocking agent is recommended and may reduce IV dosage requirements to < 0.15 mg/kg/dose.

admin_details.notes: Administer with food to enhance absorption.

ranges.notes: Administer every 6 to 8 hours. Oral dose is approximately twice the required IV dose. Use with a beta-blocking agent is recommended to enhance effect and decrease reflex tachycardia.

indications: Treatment of mild to moderate neonatal hypertension via vasodilation
Afterload reduction in patients with congestive heart failure

iv.instructions: Administer undiluted (20 mg/mL) over 30 seconds to 2 minutes. Frequent assessment of blood pressure and heart rate is required during administration.
iv.preparation: May be administered undiluted at 20 mg/mL or diluted to 1 mg/mL by adding 0.5 mL of the 20 mg/mL concentrate to 9.5 mL of preservative-free normal saline.

oral.instructions: Administered via tablets or extemporaneous liquid; note that oral bioavailability is low due to extensive first-pass metabolism and varies by acetylator phenotype.
oral.preparation: 4-mg/mL oral liquid: Prepare in a 1:1 mixture of Ora-Sweet SF/Ora-Plus (stable 2 days refrigerated). 1-mg/mL aqueous solution: Stable in mannitol and sorbitol (0.24 molar) for 21 days at room temperature. Avoid simple syrups containing dextrose, fructose, sucrose, or maltose.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of tachycardia and postural hypotension (10% to 20% in adults); requires frequent blood pressure and heart rate monitoring.
precautions.Hepatic: Subject to extensive first-pass metabolism; slow acetylator phenotypes have higher plasma concentrations and increased risk of adverse effects.
precautions.Renal: Increases renal blood flow; however, patients should be monitored for systemic effects.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Potential for GI irritation, bleeding, diarrhea, and emesis; guaiac stools are recommended to monitor for occult bleeding.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Directly relaxes smooth muscle in arteriolar resistance vessels, leading to decreased systemic vascular resistance and increased cardiac output and regional blood flow (renal, coronary, cerebral, splanchnic).

pharmacokinetics.bioavailability: Low (Oral) due to extensive first-pass metabolism
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Enzymatic metabolism by the liver and intestines via acetylation

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Oral 4-mg/mL liquid (Ora-Sweet SF/Ora-Plus) stable 2 days refrigerated or 1 day at room temperature; 1-mg/mL aqueous mannitol/sorbitol solution stable 21 days at room temperature. Unstable in dextrose, fructose, sucrose, or maltose syrups.

admin_details.notes: Administer with food (improves absorption). Do not confuse with chlorothiazide.

ranges.notes: Maintenance dosing.

indications: Improvement of pulmonary function in patients with Bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD)
Management of mild to moderate edema
Treatment of mild to moderate hypertension
Supportive care for neonates with signs of right-sided heart failure

oral.instructions: Monitor serum electrolytes, calcium, phosphorus, and glucose; track urine output and blood pressure. Use cautiously in neonates with pulmonary hypertension.
oral.preparation: Extemporaneous 5 mg/mL solution can be prepared by crushing twenty-four 25 mg spironolactone/hydroCHLOROthiazide tablets into a fine powder. Create a uniform paste with 25 mL of vehicle (1:1 Ora-Sweet/Ora-Plus, 1:1 Ora-Sweet SF/Ora-Plus, or diluted cherry syrup). Dilute to a final volume of 120 mL. Label 'shake well' and 'protect from light'.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Monitor blood pressure; use cautiously in neonates with right-sided heart failure.
precautions.Hepatic: Contraindicated in significant hepatic impairment due to risk of electrolyte imbalances.
precautions.Renal: Contraindicated in significant renal impairment; elimination half-life is dependent on GFR.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Inhibits sodium reabsorption in the distal nephron, resulting in increased urinary excretion of sodium, potassium, magnesium, chloride, phosphorus, and bicarbonate, while decreasing renal excretion of calcium.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal (proportional to Glomerular Filtration Rate)

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Extemporaneous oral suspension is stable for 60 days when stored in amber bottles (protected from light) at 5°C or 25°C.

admin_details.notes: Round the dose to the nearest 0.5 mg or 1 mg. Guideline recommendations suggest crushing the tablet formulation and mixing with a small amount of liquid just prior to administration. For granules: Pour granules directly onto the tongue, a spoon, or soft food (yogurt/puree) and swallow within 5 minutes. Give fluids immediately after. Do not chew or crush granules, do not add granules to liquid, and do not use in nasogastric or gastric tubes.

ranges.notes: Initial dose is 8 to 10 mg/m2/day divided into 3 doses. Adjust doses based on signs and symptoms of under- or overtreatment.

admin_details.notes: Administer over 30 seconds (e.g., 100 mg) up to 10 minutes (e.g., 500 mg). Preferred concentrations: 50 mg/mL for intermittent IV bolus; 1, 2, or 5 mg/mL for intermittent IV infusion.

ranges.notes: Standard dosage: 5 mg/kg/day divided into 4 doses. After 7 days, reduce to 3.75 mg/kg/day (1.25 mg/kg q8h) for 5 days, then subsequently lowering frequency by 1 dose every 5 days.
Low dose regimen: 0.5 mg/kg/dose every 12 hours for 7 days, followed by 0.5 mg/kg/day for 3 days.

admin_details.notes: Crushing tablet and mixing with liquid is preferred over liquid formulations. See administration notes for granules.

ranges.notes: Maintenance dosage: 10 to 15 mg/m2/day given in 3 divided doses.

admin_details.notes: Stress dosage for adrenal crisis in infants and preschool age children.

ranges.notes: Initial loading dose of 25 mg IV required. Then maintenance of 6.25 mg every 6 hours. Resume maintenance dosage once patient is stable.

admin_details.notes: Total daily dose 7 to 9 mg/m2/day.

ranges.notes: Based on 3 divided doses daily.
Based on 2 divided doses daily.

admin_details.notes: Total daily dose 7 to 9 mg/m2/day.

ranges.notes: Based on 3 divided doses daily.
Based on 2 divided doses daily.

admin_details.notes: For stress doses in treatment of pressor- and volume-resistant hypotension.

ranges.notes: Approximately 1 mg/kg per dose every 8 hours.
Based on 20 to 30 mg/m2/day divided into 3 doses.
Based on 20 to 30 mg/m2/day divided into 2 doses.

admin_details.notes: Used to decrease risk of CLD in ELBW infants.

ranges.notes: Initial dose 0.5 mg/kg every 12 hours for 12 days, followed by 0.25 mg/kg every 12 hours for 3 days.

indications: Replacement therapy for pediatric adrenocortical insufficiency (FDA approved).
Preferred glucocorticoid for Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) in childhood.
Treatment of pressor- and volume-resistant septic shock in newborns with adrenal insufficiency.
Individualized prevention or treatment of Chronic Lung Disease (CLD) in preterm infants (low-dose, short duration recommended).

iv.instructions: Administer bolus doses over 30 seconds (e.g., 100 mg) up to 10 minutes (e.g., 500 mg or more). Preferred concentrations are 50 mg/mL for intermittent bolus and 1, 2, or 5 mg/mL for intermittent infusion.
iv.preparation: Reconstituted solutions (50 and 125 mg/mL) may be given without further dilution. For infusion, dilute to 1 mg/mL in D5W or NS.

oral_tablet.instructions: Preferred over liquid formulations for CAH. Administer immediately after preparation.
oral_tablet.preparation: Crush tablet and mix with a small amount of liquid just prior to administration.

oral_granules.instructions: Administer directly onto the tongue, on a spoon, or sprinkled on cold/room temperature soft food (e.g., yogurt). Must be swallowed within 5 minutes to avoid bitter taste. Follow immediately with fluids (water, milk, formula) to ensure complete ingestion. Do not chew, crush, or add to liquids. Do not use in nasogastric or gastric tubes.
oral_granules.preparation: Tap capsule to ensure granules are in the lower half, twist off the top, and pour contents.

contraindications: IM administration in patients with idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura.
Live or live-attenuated vaccines in patients receiving immunosuppressive doses.
A-Hydrocort formulation in premature infants (contains benzyl alcohol).

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of hypertension and salt/water retention; enhances vascular reactivity to norepinephrine and angiotensin II.
precautions.Renal: Increased renal calcium excretion; monitor electrolytes.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Increased risk of GI perforation, especially with concomitant NSAID or indomethacin use, or in patients with diverticulitis, peptic ulcers, or fresh intestinal anastomoses.

serious: Adrenal crisis upon sudden discontinuation or stress
Growth retardation and inhibition of bone growth
Fine motor and language delays associated with prolonged high-dose courses

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Primary adrenal corticosteroid with glucocorticoid effects; increases vascular reactivity to vasoactive substances via adrenergic receptor expression; stimulates gluconeogenesis and glycogen deposition while diminishing peripheral glucose utilization.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal (calcium excretion); Protein binding ≥ 90%

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Oral granules must be consumed within 5 minutes of preparation. Tablets crushed in liquid must be used immediately.

admin_details.notes: Administer by intermittent IV infusion. Contraindicated in history of asthma/NSAID allergy or CABG surgery. Precautions: risk of CV thrombotic events, hypertension, fluid retention, GI bleeding, renal toxicity, and hyperkalemia. Monitor renal function and for signs of bleeding.

ranges.notes: Standard Dose Maintenance. Initial loading dose of 10 mg/kg required. Followed by maintenance doses of 5 mg/kg at 24 and 48 hours after the first dose. A second course has been used.

admin_details.notes: May be mixed with 0.5 mL milk and administered via oro-gastric tube. Contraindicated in history of asthma/NSAID allergy or CABG surgery. Monitor for GI bleeding, renal impairment, and electrolyte imbalances.

ranges.notes: Standard Dose Maintenance. Initial loading dose of 10 mg/kg required. Followed by maintenance doses of 5 mg/kg at 24 and 48 hours after the first dose. A second course has been used.
High Dose Maintenance. Initial loading dose of 15 to 20 mg/kg required. Then 7.5 to 10 mg/kg orally per day for a total of 3 doses.

indications: Closure of hemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) in select preterm newborns
Reduction of fever in children 6 months of age and older
Management of mild to moderate pain in children 6 months of age and older
Management of moderate to severe pain as an adjunct to opioid analgesics in children 6 months of age and older (IV)
Treatment of signs and symptoms of juvenile arthritis

iv.instructions: Administer by intermittent IV infusion over 15 minutes.
iv.preparation: Dilute to a final concentration of 4 mg/mL or less.

oral.instructions: Shake oral suspension well before using. Can be administered via oro-gastric tube.
oral.preparation: May be mixed with 0.5 mL milk for administration via oro-gastric tube.

contraindications: History of asthma, urticaria, or allergic-type reactions after taking aspirin or other NSAIDs
Use in the setting of coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery

precautions.Cardiovascular: Increased risk of serious thrombotic events (MI and stroke); risk of new/worsened hypertension, fluid retention, and edema. Avoid in recent MI or severe heart failure.
precautions.Hepatic: Potential for ALT/AST elevations (3x ULN) and rare fatal hepatic injury/failure; discontinue if liver disease symptoms develop.
precautions.Renal: Risk of renal papillary necrosis and toxicity, especially in patients with preexisting impairment, dehydration, or concomitant ACE inhibitor/diuretic use.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Increased risk of serious GI bleeding, ulceration, and perforation; risk is higher with advanced liver disease, anticoagulants, or prolonged use.

serious: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)
Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) with anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic activity.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal (implied via toxicity/monitoring data)

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Oral suspension should be shaken well before use. No specific stability data provided for IV dilutions.

admin_details.notes: Administer at a concentration of 5 mg/mL or less. Renal Adjustment: Not recommended in pediatric patients weighing less than 30 kg with renal impairment.

ranges.notes: Usual dose for weight 2 kg or less.
Usual dose for weight 2 kg or less.
Usual dose for weight 1.5 kg or more.
Usual dose for weight 1.5 kg or more.

admin_details.notes: Used as part of a triple therapy regimen. Administer at concentration of 5 mg/mL or less.

ranges.notes: Duration: 2 to 3 weeks or more until stable. Continue antimicrobial course of prophylaxis for up to 60 days from onset of illness.
Duration: 2 to 3 weeks or more until stable. Continue antimicrobial course of prophylaxis for up to 60 days from onset of illness.

admin_details.notes: Used as part of a combination regimen. Administer at concentration of 5 mg/mL or less.

ranges.notes: Duration: 2 to 3 weeks or more until stable. Continue antimicrobial course of prophylaxis for up to 60 days from onset.
Duration: 2 to 3 weeks or more until stable. Continue antimicrobial course of prophylaxis for up to 60 days from onset.
Duration: 2 to 3 weeks or more until stable. Continue antimicrobial course of prophylaxis for up to 60 days from onset.
Duration: 2 to 3 weeks or more until stable. Continue antimicrobial course of prophylaxis for up to 60 days from onset.

indications: Systemic Anthrax: Preferred component of combination IV therapy for both non-meningitis and meningitis-involved cases.
Lower Respiratory Tract Infections: Caused by S. aureus, Acinetobacter, Enterobacter, E. coli, H. influenzae, Klebsiella, and S. marcescens.
Urinary Tract Infections: Complicated and uncomplicated infections caused by various gram-positive and gram-negative pathogens.
Intra-abdominal Infections: Effective against broad-spectrum aerobic and anaerobic organisms; recommended as a single agent for pediatric extra-biliary infections.
Gynecologic Infections: Treatment of infections involving E. faecalis, S. aureus, B. fragilis, and other susceptible organisms.
Bacterial Septicemia: Treatment of blood-borne infections caused by S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and Enterobacteriaceae.
Bone and Joint Infections: Management of infections involving E. faecalis, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa.
Skin and Skin Structure Infections: Broad-spectrum coverage including S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and various anaerobes.
Endocarditis: Caused by penicillinase-producing strains of S. aureus.

iv.instructions: Administer via IV infusion over 20 to 30 minutes. Ensure a final concentration of 5 mg/mL or less.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute powder with a compatible diluent and shake well. Transfer vial contents to 100 mL of Normal Saline (NS) or D5W. For ADD-Vantage® vials, use 100 mL of the provided ADD-Vantage® diluent. Total dose must not exceed a concentration of 5 mg/mL.

im.instructions: No specific IM administration instructions provided in the monograph text.
im.preparation: Reconstitute with lidocaine (not specified in detail, but referenced in safety constraints).

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to imipenem, cilastatin, or any product component.
IM form: Hypersensitivity to amide-type local anesthetics.
IM form: Patients with severe shock or heart block.

precautions.Cardiovascular: IM administration is contraindicated in patients with severe shock or heart block due to lidocaine diluent.
precautions.Hepatic: Elevated hepatic transaminases observed; monitor liver function periodically.
precautions.Renal: Increased risk of seizures in patients with renal dysfunction; clearance is directly dependent on renal function.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Diarrhea reported in more than 5% of patients; risk of antibiotic-associated colitis.
precautions.Neurological: High risk of seizures, particularly in pediatric patients with CNS infections or meningitis; avoid pediatric use for CNS-related conditions.

serious: Seizures (especially in CNS pathology or renal impairment)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Bactericidal carbapenem that inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis. Combined 1:1 with cilastatin, a renal dipeptidase inhibitor that prevents renal metabolism of imipenem.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal (Clearance directly correlates with renal function)

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: After reconstitution with compatible diluent: stable for 4 hours at room temperature and 24 hours under refrigeration.

admin_details.notes: Gamastan S/D. Do not administer by subQ or IV. Do not administer in the gluteal region. Administer in the anterolateral aspects of the upper thigh and deltoid muscle of the upper arm. Do not administer at the same time as measles vaccine. Give within 6 days of exposure.

ranges.notes: Administer within 6 days of exposure.

admin_details.notes: IM and IV products are not interchangeable. Selection of product depends on osmolarity, pH, viscosity, volume, and sodium/sugar content. Rate varies by product brand. In renal impairment: reduce administration rate, use lowest concentration, and consider non-sucrose products.

ranges.notes: Administer within 6 days of exposure.

admin_details.notes: Total serum bilirubin at or above escalation of care threshold. Rate varies by brand; see specific product tables.

ranges.notes: May repeat in 12 hours if necessary.

admin_details.notes: Rate of administration varies by product (e.g., 0.5 mg/kg/min to 8 mg/kg/min depending on brand and tolerance).

ranges.notes: Administer daily for 2 doses.

indications: Isoimmune hemolytic disease in infants refractory to phototherapy.
Post-exposure measles prophylaxis in non-immune individuals (specifically immunocompromised hosts, bone marrow transplant recipients, and HIV-infected patients).
Treatment of primary immunodeficiency diseases (PIDD) in pediatric and adult patients.
Treatment of idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP).
Treatment of chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP).
Treatment of Kawasaki syndrome.
Management of neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia (conflicting evidence).
Adjunctive treatment for sepsis in neonates weighing <1500g (limited evidence).

im.instructions: Administer only via the anterolateral aspects of the upper thigh or the deltoid muscle of the upper arm. Do not administer in the gluteal region. Do not administer simultaneously with measles vaccine. Avoid subcutaneous or intravenous administration.
im.preparation: Use as supplied in 2 mL or 10 mL vials. Ensure baseline blood viscosity is assessed in patients at risk for hyperviscosity.

iv.instructions: Selection is based on osmolarity, pH, viscosity, and sugar/sodium content. Infusion rates are product-specific; monitor vital signs closely during the first and second infusions. Ensure adequate hydration before administration. Use a separate line; do not mix with other medications.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute lyophilized products with supplied diluent; swirl gently and do not shake. Bring refrigerated products to room temperature before administration. Do not freeze.

reconstitution_table.initial_rate: 0.5 mg/kg/min for 10 min; increase to max 6 mg/kg/min.
reconstitution_table.diluent_compatibility: Not recommended to be mixed with any solutions.
reconstitution_table.storage: Refrigerate; use immediately after entering vial.

reconstitution_table.initial_rate: 0.5 mg/kg/min (3% solution); increase to max 3 mg/kg/min.
reconstitution_table.storage: Room temperature; stable 24h refrigerated after reconstitution.

reconstitution_table.initial_rate: 0.01 mL/kg/min; increase to max 0.1 mL/kg/min (5%) or 0.08 mL/kg/min (10%).
reconstitution_table.diluent_compatibility: Not recommended to be mixed with IV solutions.

reconstitution_table.initial_rate: 0.5 mL/kg/hr for 30 min; increase to max 5 mL/kg/hr.
0.5 mL/kg/hr (5% solution); increase to max 4 mL/kg/hr.
reconstitution_table.diluent_compatibility: Do not mix with other IV solutions.
reconstitution_table.storage: Room temp; stable 24h refrigerated after reconstitution.

reconstitution_table.initial_rate: 0.5 mg/kg/min; increase to max 8 mg/kg/min (PI) or 4 mg/kg/min (ITP).
reconstitution_table.storage: Room temperature; use pooled vials within 8 hours.

subcutaneous.instructions: Indicated for pediatric primary immunodeficiency disease in patients 2 years or older.

contraindications: Anaphylaxis or severe systemic reaction to human immunoglobulins or components (e.g., polysorbate 80).
Hyperprolinemia (Type I or II) for L-proline stabilized products (Hizentra, Privigen).
IgA deficiency with known anti-IgA antibodies.
Severe thrombocytopenia or coagulation disorders (for IM administration).

precautions.Cardiovascular: High-dose regimens may lead to fluid overload; thrombosis risk exists regardless of risk factors; monitor patients with cardiovascular risk factors closely.
precautions.Renal: Acute renal dysfunction, osmotic nephrosis, and acute renal failure (especially with sucrose-containing products); increased risk with nephrotoxic drugs or preexisting insufficiency.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Potential risk of necrotizing enterocolitis in infants treated for isoimmune hemolytic jaundice.

serious: Life-threatening hypoglycemia (due to maltose interference with glucose tests)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Plasma-derived, concentrated IgG antibodies providing passive immunity and modulating immune response through various pathways, containing >92% IgG monomers and normal subclass distribution.

pharmacokinetics.half_life: Total IgG titers remain elevated for approximately 10 days in septic neonates.
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primary routes not specified; metabolized as endogenous IgG.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Preservative-free; most products must be used immediately after opening; pooled vials are typically stable for 8 hours; refrigerated reconstituted products may be stable up to 24 hours depending on brand.

admin_details.notes: Infuse solution over 20 to 30 minutes. Give at 12- to 24-hour intervals with close monitoring of urine output. If anuria or severe oliguria occurs, subsequent doses should be delayed. Maximum two courses.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose (2nd and 3rd doses). Initial loading dose of 0.2 mg/kg required.
Maintenance Dose (2nd and 3rd doses). Initial loading dose of 0.2 mg/kg required.
Maintenance Dose (2nd and 3rd doses). Initial loading dose of 0.2 mg/kg required.
Longer treatment course option.

admin_details.notes: Infuse solution over 20 to 30 minutes.

ranges.notes: Premature infants: Beginning within the first 6 to 24 hours of birth. 0.1 mg/kg every 24 hours may attenuate potential reductions in urinary output.

indications: Treatment of hemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) in premature infants weighing 500 to 1750 g.
Prophylaxis for intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH) in settings with high incidence rates.
Prevention of early, severe pulmonary hemorrhage in high-risk preterm infants.
Short-term reduction in the frequency of symptomatic PDA and the need for surgical ligation.

iv.instructions: Infuse the solution over 20 to 30 minutes. Rapid infusions (less than 5 minutes) should be avoided as they are associated with reduced organ blood flow. Continuous infusion is generally recommended against by the manufacturer due to stability and sterility concerns of the preservative-free formulation.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute the 1-mg lyophilized powder vial with 1 to 2 mL of preservative-free Normal Saline (NS) or Sterile Water for Injection to achieve a concentration of 0.5 to 1 mg/mL. Note that the drug is insoluble in solutions with a pH less than 6.

contraindications: Setting of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) surgery

precautions.Cardiovascular: Increased risk of serious cardiovascular thrombotic events, including fatal myocardial infarction and stroke; risk may increase with duration of use.
precautions.Renal: Risk of significant decrease in urine output (oliguria), decreased GFR, and increased serum creatinine; monitor for hyponatremia and hypokalemia.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Increased risk of serious GI adverse events including bleeding, ulceration, and fatal perforation; risk is higher if used concurrently with corticosteroids.

common: Transient increase in serum creatinine and BUN
serious: Reduction in cerebral, renal, and mesenteric blood flow

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits prostaglandin synthesis to exert anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic effects; it also decreases cerebral, renal, and gastrointestinal blood flow.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Metabolized in the liver to inactive compounds; excreted via urine and feces.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted solution (0.5 mg/mL in sterile water) is stable for 14 days in polypropylene syringes at 2-6°C or 21-25°C. In the original glass vial, it is stable for 14 days at 2-6°C and 12 days at 21-25°C.

admin_details.notes: Should not be given as a direct, undiluted intravenous injection. Add the required dose to not less than 100 mL of dextrose, saline, or similar infusion solutions.

ranges.notes: Total dose (1.5 mL) consists of 1.2 mL from Vial 1 and 0.3 mL from Vial 2. Supplemental vitamin A may be required for low-birth weight infants.
Total dose (3.25 mL) consists of 2.6 mL from Vial 1 and 0.65 mL from Vial 2. Supplemental vitamin A may be required for low-birth weight infants.
Total dose (5 mL) consists of 4 mL from Vial 1 and 1 mL from Vial 2. Indicated for infants and children up to 11 years of age.

indications: Multivitamin maintenance for infants and children up to 11 years of age receiving parenteral nutrition.
Prevention of nutrient depletion in patients in stress situations requiring IV administration (e.g., surgery, burns, fractures, trauma, severe infection, and comatose states).

iv.instructions: Do not administer as a direct, undiluted intravenous injection. Inspect visually for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. Exposure to light should be minimized.
iv.preparation: Aseptically transfer the contents of Vial 1 (4 mL) and Vial 2 (1 mL) into at least 100 mL of compatible infusion solution (e.g., dextrose or saline).

precautions.Renal: Prolonged administration in patients with renal impairment or preterm neonates increases the risk of aluminum toxicity (CNS and bone toxicity).
precautions.Hematologic: Vitamin K content may antagonize the hypoprothrombinemic response of anticoagulant drugs.
precautions.General: Contains polysorbates, which are associated with E-Ferol syndrome.

serious: Aluminum toxicity leading to central nervous system and bone toxicity.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Provides a comprehensive combination of fat-soluble and water-soluble vitamins to maintain normal vitamin blood levels and prevent tissue depletion.

availability: Vial 1 (4 mL): Fat-soluble and certain water-soluble vitamins
Vial 2 (1 mL): Biotin, Folic Acid, and Vitamin B12
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Resulting solution is ready for immediate use after dilution. Discard any unused portion. Store refrigerated between 2°C and 8°C (36°F to 46°F).

admin_details.notes: Administered via continuous SubQ infusion pump therapy. Total daily dose typically ranges from 0.15 to 1 units/kg/day.

ranges.notes: Basal rate expressed as units/kg/day.
Basal rate expressed as units/hr.

admin_details.notes: Standard neonate concentrations are 0.1 unit/mL and 0.5 unit/mL. To reduce adsorption: fill IV tubing and wait 20 minutes (preconditioning) OR run 20 mL priming volume through tubing prior to initiation.

ranges.notes: Continuous IV infusion. Titrate dose using blood glucose concentration/reagent strips.

admin_details.notes: Standard concentrations: 0.1 unit/mL and 0.5 unit/mL. Saturate plastic tubing binding sites by preconditioning (20 min wait) or 20 mL priming volume.

ranges.notes: Continuous IV infusion. Give in combination with 0.5 g/kg/hour of dextrose. Adjust based on serum glucose and potassium concentrations.

indications: Neonatal diabetes mellitus management via continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (pump therapy)
Persistent glucose intolerance in Very Low Birth Weight (VLBW) infants
Glycemic control in critically ill pediatric patients (though routine use in non-diabetic VLBW infants to promote growth is not warranted)
Treatment of hyperkalemia in combination with dextrose
Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes mellitus in pediatric patients
Glycemic control in patients requiring >200 units/day (U-500 concentration)

iv.instructions: Only regular insulin may be administered intravenously. Recommended standard neonate concentrations are 0.1 unit/mL and 0.5 unit/mL. To minimize adsorption losses, perform 'preconditioning' by filling tubing and waiting 20 minutes, or prime the tubing with 20 mL of the infusion solution prior to initiation.
iv.preparation: Dilute regular insulin in compatible solution to a concentration of 0.05 to 1 unit/mL (polypropylene bags) or 0.1 to 1 unit/mL (PVC bags). For initial preparation, create a 10 units/mL dilution with sterile water before further dilution in compatible infusion fluid.

subcutaneous.instructions: Administered via multiple daily injections or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (pump). Pump therapy is preferred for neonates to allow for micro-dosing, temporary basal rates, and square-wave boluses for slow feeders. Rotate injection sites to reduce risk of lipodystrophy or cutaneous amyloidosis.
subcutaneous.preparation: For neonatal use, dilute with sterile water or Normal Saline (NS) to a concentration of 0.5 or 1 unit/mL.

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to human regular insulin or any of its components

precautions.Cardiovascular: Concomitant use of PPAR-gamma agonists may cause fluid retention and lead to new or worsening heart failure.
precautions.Hepatic: Patients with hepatic impairment may require more frequent dose adjustments and increased glucose monitoring.
precautions.Renal: Patients with renal impairment may require more frequent dose adjustments and increased glucose monitoring.

serious: Medication errors with U-500 concentration (risk of death)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Enhances cellular uptake of glucose, amino acids, and potassium; promotes glycogenesis and fat synthesis while inhibiting lipolysis and gluconeogenesis.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Degraded in the liver and kidney

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Unopened vials/pens: Refrigerated (2-8°C) until expiry or RT (<30°C) for 28-40 days. Opened vials: Refrigerated or RT for up to 40 days. Opened KwikPens: Room temperature only for up to 28 days (do not refrigerate).

admin_details.notes: Use chlorofluorocarbon free preparations. Simulated neonatal lung models suggest greater delivery when using a spacer with the MDI. Place spacer device in the inspiratory limb of the ventilator circuit.

ranges.notes: Dose corresponds to 2 puffs (34 mcg) to 4 puffs (68 mcg). Administer every 6 to 8 hours. Optimal dose in neonates has yet to be determined; therapeutic margin appears wide.

admin_details.notes: Use chlorofluorocarbon free preparations. Specifically studied using jet nebulizer.

ranges.notes: Dose corresponds to 75 to 175 mcg. Administer every 6 to 8 hours. Optimal dose in neonates has yet to be determined; therapeutic margin appears wide.

indications: Primary treatment of chronic obstructive pulmonary diseases (COPD)
Adjunctive treatment of acute bronchospasm
Note: Not indicated for the treatment of bronchiolitis

inhalation.instructions: Administer via metered-dose inhaler or nebulizer; monitor degree of bronchospasm to assess efficacy; ensure administration prevents direct contact with eyes to avoid ocular complications.
inhalation.preparation: Solution for inhalation may be mixed with albuterol or metaproterenol if used within 1 hour of preparation.

precautions.Ocular: Direct contact with eyes may cause temporary blurring of vision, eye pain, or precipitation of narrow-angle glaucoma.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A quaternary ammonium derivative of atropine that acts as an anticholinergic; it induces bronchodilation by antagonizing acetylcholine at muscarinic receptor sites, primarily in the large airways.

pharmacokinetics.half_life: Peak effect: 1 to 2 hours; Duration: 4 to 6 hours (pediatrics)
pharmacokinetics.bioavailability: Limited systemic absorption through lung tissue

availability: Metered-dose inhaler (Atrovent® HFA): 12.9-g canister (200 actuations; 17 mcg/actuation)
Solution for inhalation: 0.02% (200 mcg/mL) in 2.5-mL sterile vials
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store solution vials at room temperature in the original foil pouch; protect from light. Mixed solutions (with albuterol/metaproterenol) must be used within 1 hour.

admin_details.notes: May be added to peripheral nutrition solutions. The solution must contain an amino acid final concentration of at least 2%.

ranges.notes: Administered as a continuous infusion.

indications: Iron supplementation in patients with oral iron intolerance
Iron supplementation for patients receiving erythropoietin therapy

iv.instructions: May be administered via continuous infusion in peripheral nutrition solutions; monitor for rust-colored precipitates which indicate solution instability.
iv.preparation: When added to parenteral nutrition, the final solution must maintain an amino acid concentration of at least 2%.

im.instructions: Administer with caution; 50-mg doses in infants are associated with an increased risk of infection.

precautions.Hepatic: Radiolabeled iron localizes to the liver and spleen prior to RBC incorporation.
precautions.General: Black Box Warning: Fatal anaphylactic reactions have occurred; a test dose is mandatory prior to administration. Resuscitation equipment and trained personnel must be immediately available. Patients with multiple drug allergies are at increased risk.

common: Delayed reactions (5%): myalgia, arthralgia, phlebitis, and lymphadenopathy
serious: Immediate serious allergic reactions (0.7% risk with rapid injection)
Increased risk of infection in infants (IM route)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A complex of ferric hydroxide and low molecular mass dextran that functions as a protective lipophilic colloid; the iron localizes to the liver and spleen before being incorporated into red blood cell hemoglobin. It also inhibits spontaneous peroxide generation in Dex/AA solutions.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Liver and spleen localization; 40% transferrin binding within 11 hours

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store at room temperature; note that iron dextran products are not interchangeable.

admin_details.notes: Continuous IV infusion. Maximum concentration 20 mcg/mL. Concentrations as low as 2 mcg/mL (0.002 mg/mL) have been used. Acidosis should be corrected before infusion.

ranges.notes: Dosage often titrated according to heart rate. Correct acidosis before infusion.

indications: Increase cardiac output in patients with cardiovascular shock
Pulmonary vasodilator (older infants)

iv.instructions: Administer via continuous infusion. Titrate based on clinical response. Requires continuous monitoring of vital signs, intra-arterial blood pressure, and preferably central venous pressure (CVP). Perform periodic blood glucose monitoring.
iv.preparation: Standard maximum concentration is 20 mcg/mL. Dilute with compatible solutions (D5W, D10W, or NS). Calculate volume needed: (Desired concentration [mg/mL] x final volume [mL]) / vial concentration [0.2 mg/mL]. Example: To prepare 50 mL of 10 mcg/mL solution, add 2.5 mL of drug to 47.5 mL of diluent.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of severe tachycardia which may precipitate congestive heart failure (CHF); may decrease venous return and cause systemic vasodilation.
precautions.Metabolic: Stimulates insulin secretion; risk of hypoglycemia requiring periodic blood glucose monitoring.
precautions.Respiratory: Potential to cause hypoxemia by increasing intrapulmonary shunting.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: β-receptor stimulant and sympathomimetic. Increases cardiac output primarily by increasing heart rate and secondarily via increased contractility. Provides afterload reduction through β2-mediated effects on arterioles and stimulates insulin secretion.

admin_details.notes: Screen for Hepatitis B virus prior to initiating therapy. Can be given without regard to meals.

ranges.notes: Renal Impairment: Dose reduction and/or increase in dosing interval should be considered.

indications: Treatment of HIV-1 infection in combination with other antiretroviral agents in children 3 months and older.
Preferred dual-NRTI component (with abacavir or zidovudine) for neonatal HIV management.
Initial therapy for newborns at high risk for perinatal HIV transmission (combined with zidovudine and nevirapine or raltegravir).
Prevention of maternal-fetal HIV transmission in infants born to women who received no antenatal ART.

oral.instructions: May be administered without regard to meals. Monitor viral load more frequently when using the oral solution due to potential for lower plasma exposure compared to tablets.

contraindications: Use of Epivir-HBV® formulations for the treatment of HIV-1 (contains lower doses unsuitable for HIV management).
Co-administration with emtricitabine (due to similar resistance patterns and lack of additive benefit).

precautions.Hepatic: Lactic acidosis and severe hepatomegaly with steatosis (including fatal cases) reported, particularly in women and obese patients. Severe acute exacerbation of Hepatitis B (HBV) may occur upon discontinuation; monitor hepatic function for several months post-therapy.
precautions.Renal: Clearance is significantly reduced in neonates <1 week old compared to older children; dose reduction is required in patients with renal impairment.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Risk of pancreatitis; use with caution in patients with prior nucleoside exposure or known risk factors. Discontinue immediately if symptoms of pancreatitis occur.

serious: Autoimmune disorders (e.g., Graves' disease, polymyositis, Guillain-Barre syndrome)
Transient renal insufficiency associated with dehydration

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: A synthetic nucleoside analog that inhibits HIV and HBV replication by interfering with viral reverse transcriptase after being intracellularly converted to its active metabolite.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily renally excreted as unchanged drug

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store formulations at room temperature.

admin_details.notes: Mix capsule contents in 40 mL of apple juice for NG tube administration. Do not use other liquids. Flush NG tube with additional apple juice after administration. Teva brand delayed-release orally disintegrating tablets should NOT be used with feeding tubes due to risk of clogging and tube replacement.

ranges.notes: Data for partial doses via neonatal feeding tubes are lacking. 7.5 mg doses have been reported to clog 5 French and 6 French feeding tubes; 8 French tubes may be successful.

indications: First-line treatment for erosive esophagitis in infants and children with GERD.
Treatment of GERD symptoms (recommended duration 4 to 8 weeks).
Short-term diagnostic trial (4 to 8 weeks) for children presenting with typical GERD symptoms.
Not recommended for crying, irritability, or visible regurgitation in healthy term infants.
Not recommended for extraesophageal symptoms (e.g., cough, wheezing) unless GERD is also present.
Use with extreme caution in preterm infants for GER as risks typically outweigh benefits.

oral.instructions: Capsule contents may be administered via NG tube using apple juice; flush with apple juice post-administration. ODT formulations may be used but carry risks of tube clogging. Monitor for symptomatic improvement within 3 days.
oral.preparation: Capsule contents should only be mixed with 40 mL of apple juice for NG administration. For extemporaneous suspension: mix ten 30-mg capsules with 100 mL of 8.4% sodium bicarbonate solution for 30 minutes. For compounding kits: add suspension to powder in two stages, shaking well after each addition.

enteral_tube.instructions: Use caution with small-bore tubes; 7.5 mg ODT doses may clog 5 French or 6 French tubes. Avoid Teva brand ODTs in patients with feeding tubes due to high risk of clumping and blockage requiring emergency intervention.
enteral_tube.preparation: Do not use liquids other than apple juice for capsule contents via NG tube. ODTs should be fully disintegrated; however, be aware of clumping risks in tube walls.

precautions.Hepatic: Extensively metabolized by the liver; mild transaminase elevations reported with long-term pediatric use.
precautions.Renal: Risk of acute tubulointerstitial nephritis; may occur at any time and requires immediate discontinuation if suspected.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Increased risk of Clostridium difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and necrotizing enterocolitis in infants/children.
precautions.Dermatologic: Risk of severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCARs) including SJS, TEN, DRESS, and AGEP; requires discontinuation.
precautions.Endocrine_Metabolic: Potential for hypomagnesemia (long-term use), Vitamin B12 deficiency, and false-positive neuroendocrine tumor results due to increased CgA levels.
precautions.Immunologic: Risk of new-onset or systemic/cutaneous lupus erythematosus; increased risk of sepsis and respiratory infections in pediatric patients.
precautions.Special_Populations: ODT formulations contain phenylalanine; use caution in patients with phenylketonuria.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Suppresses gastric acid secretion by inhibiting the hydrogen-potassium ATPase enzyme system (the 'proton pump') at the secretory surface of the gastric parietal cell.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Hepatic metabolism via CYP 2C19 and 3A4

availability: 3 mg/mL compounding kits (90 mL, 150 mL, 300 mL)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Commercial compounding kits: 30 days refrigerated (2-8°C). Sodium bicarbonate extemporaneous suspension: 7 days refrigerated (3-4°C) or 48 hours at room temperature (20-22°C).

admin_details.notes: Dilute in 100 mL or less of compatible diluent (do not exceed 15 mg/mL). A standard concentration is 5 mg/mL or 15 mg/mL for intermittent IV administration.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose. Calculated from 40 to 60 mg/kg/day divided 3 times daily. Initial loading dose of 40 mg/kg IV required; if required, a second loading dose of 20 mg/kg IV may be administered. Primarily eliminated renally.

admin_details.notes: May be given without regards to feedings.

ranges.notes: Maintenance Dose. Calculated from 40 to 60 mg/kg/day divided 3 times daily. Initial loading dose of 40 mg/kg IV recommended before starting maintenance. Primarily eliminated renally.

admin_details.notes: Dilute in 100 mL or less of compatible diluent (do not exceed 15 mg/mL).

ranges.notes: Median dose used in observational study was 20 mg/kg/day. Primarily eliminated renally.

indications: First- or second-line treatment for neonatal seizures.
Prophylaxis for early posttraumatic seizures (within 7 days) following traumatic brain injury in pediatric patients.
Monotherapy or adjunctive therapy for partial onset seizures in patients 1 month of age or older.
Adjunctive therapy for myoclonic seizures in patients 12 years or older with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy.
Adjunctive therapy for primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures in patients 6 years or older with idiopathic generalized epilepsy.

iv.instructions: Administer via intermittent infusion over 15 minutes. Standard concentrations for administration are 5 mg/mL or 15 mg/mL.
iv.preparation: Dilute in 100 mL or less of compatible diluent (NS, LR, or D5W). Do not exceed a maximum concentration of 15 mg/mL.

oral.instructions: May be administered without regard to food intake.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Increased diastolic blood pressure risk in pediatric patients aged 1 month to <4 years.
precautions.Renal: Dosage adjustments required for renal impairment; drug clearance is linearly related to creatinine clearance.
precautions.Dermatologic: Serious reactions including Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) reported; requires immediate discontinuation.
precautions.Hematologic: Risk of decreased WBC, RBC, hemoglobin, and hematocrit; potential for agranulocytosis, pancytopenia, and thrombocytopenia.
precautions.Neurologic: Common reports of somnolence, fatigue, and asthenia; mandatory gradual withdrawal to avoid increased seizure frequency or status epilepticus.
precautions.Immunologic: Risk of anaphylaxis or angioedema which may occur at any time during treatment; requires permanent discontinuation.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: The exact mechanism of action by which levetiracetam exerts its antiepileptic effect is unknown.

pharmacokinetics.half_life: 8.9 to 15.6 hours in neonates (decreases with postnatal age)
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily renal; clearance is highly dependent on creatinine clearance and postnatal age.

admin_details.notes: Administer on empty stomach, 0.5 to 1 hour before breakfast (solution: 15 mins before). Separate by 4 hours from antacids, calcium, iron, or sucralfate. Tablets: Crush and suspend in 5-10 mL water; do not use formula or other liquids.

ranges.notes: Initial dose. Individualize based on TSH and T4 levels. Peak therapeutic effect may take 4 to 6 weeks.

admin_details.notes: Do not exceed an IV administration rate of 100 mcg/min. Do not add to any other IV solution.

ranges.notes: Initial IV dose. At risk for cardiac failure: Consider a lower initial dose.

indications: Replacement or supplemental therapy for primary, secondary, and tertiary congenital or acquired hypothyroidism in pediatric patients.
Adjunctive treatment for thyrotropin-dependent well-differentiated thyroid cancer.

Note: Limitations include lack of indication for suppression of benign thyroid nodules or nontoxic diffuse goiter in iodine-deficient patients, or for hypothyroidism during subacute thyroiditis recovery.

oral.instructions: Administer as a single daily dose on an empty stomach, 0.5 to 1 hour before breakfast (tablets) or 15 minutes before breakfast (solution). Separate administration from interfering drugs (antacids, bile acid sequestrants, calcium carbonate, iron, orlistat, sucralfate) by at least 4 hours. Evaluate dose adjustments if administered within 1 hour of food intake.
oral.preparation: Tablets may be crushed and suspended in 5-10 mL of water for pediatric patients; do not use other liquids or formula. Solution may be administered directly into the mouth via single-unit ampule or mixed into water and consumed immediately. Capsules must not be crushed or suspended.

reconstitution_table.method: Crush 100 mcg tablets in glycerol and add sterile water to volume.
Crush 200 mcg tablets; mix with 1:1 Simple syrup NF and 1% methylcellulose, or 1:1 OraSweet and OraPlus.
Crush tablets and mix with 40% glycerol and distilled water (preservative-free).

iv.instructions: Administer at a rate not exceeding 100 mcg/min. Do not add to or mix with other IV solutions.
iv.preparation: Reconstitute lyophilized powder vials (100 or 500 mcg) with 5 mL of Normal Saline (NS) only. Solution is available in single-dose vials (20, 40, or 100 mcg/mL).

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to thyroid hormone or any product component.
Uncorrected adrenal insufficiency (risk of acute adrenal crisis).

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of new or worsening arrhythmias, angina, or tachycardia; lower initial doses required for patients with underlying cardiovascular disease or coronary artery disease undergoing surgery.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Myxedema coma may result in unpredictable GI absorption; IV administration is required.

serious: Premature craniosynostosis and accelerated bone age (prolonged overtreatment)
Life-threatening toxicity if used for weight loss (Black Box Warning)

Terminal_Injection_Site_Incompatibility: All other IV solutions (Instruction: Do not add to any other IV solution)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Levothyroxine (T4) acts as a prohormone; it is converted via tissue deiodination to T3, the active metabolite, which regulates metabolic processes.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Primarily urine; approximately 20% in stool.

availability: IV Lyophilized Powder: 100 mcg, 500 mcg vials
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted IV powder: 4 hours (preservative-free). Oral 15 mcg/mL suspension: 10 days (refrigerated). Oral 25 mcg/mL suspension: 14 days (refrigerated) or 7 days (room temp). Oral 25 mcg/mL glycerol-based: 8 days (refrigerated).

admin_details.notes: Concentration for IV push dose is 1 to 20 mg/mL. For continuous infusion, dilute to 0.8 to 8 mg/mL (premixed solutions are 4 to 8 mg/mL). Desired final concentration (mg/mL) x final volume (mL) = mg of drug to add.

ranges.notes: Maintenance IV infusion. Premature neonates should receive lowest dosage. Initial bolus dose: 0.5 to 1 mg/kg IV push over 5 minutes. Repeat every 10 minutes as necessary to control arrhythmia. Maximum total bolus dose should not exceed 5 mg/kg.

indications: Short-term control of ventricular arrhythmias
Treatment of ventricular tachycardia
Management of premature ventricular contractions
Treatment of arrhythmias resulting from digitalis intoxication

iv.instructions: Administer IV push at concentrations of 1 to 20 mg/mL. For continuous infusion, maintain concentration between 0.8 to 8 mg/mL. Continuous ECG, heart rate, and blood pressure monitoring is required. Assess level of consciousness and observe for seizures.
iv.preparation: Dilute 10 mg (0.5 mL of 2% solution) in 9.5 mL NS or D5W for a 1 mg/mL bolus concentration. For infusions, use premixed solutions (4-8 mg/mL) or calculate dosage using (Desired mg/mL x volume = total mg needed). Use only preservative-free and epinephrine-free lidocaine.

contraindications: Severe degrees of sinoatrial, atrioventricular, or intraventricular block
Known hypersensitivity to amide-type local anesthetics

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of accelerated ventricular rate in atrial fibrillation/flutter; potential for complete heart block in patients with sinus bradycardia or incomplete block.
precautions.Hepatic: Increased risk of lidocaine toxicity due to reduced drug clearance.
precautions.Renal: Increased risk of lidocaine toxicity with impaired renal function.
precautions.Hematologic: Risk of methemoglobinemia, especially in patients with G6PD deficiency, infants <6 months, or concurrent oxidant exposure.
precautions.Metabolic: Potential for malignant hyperthermia; requires immediate discontinuation.
precautions.Special_Populations: Viscous lidocaine is contraindicated for teething pain; associated with seizures and death in infants.

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Type 1b antiarrhythmic agent that stabilizes neuronal membranes and inhibits sodium channels.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Liver metabolism to active metabolites; approximately 30% excreted unchanged in neonates.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store at room temperature (20 to 25°C). Avoid excessive heat and protect from light.

admin_details.notes: Loading dose bolus concentration not to exceed 20 mg/mL. For continuous infusion, dilute to 0.8-8 mg/mL or use premixed 4-8 mg/mL.

ranges.notes: Initial maintenance dose for normothermic term newborns. Initial loading dose of 2 mg/kg IV bolus over 10 minutes required immediately prior to infusion. Caution: Preterm and hypothermia newborns are at risk for drug accumulation; precise dosing is uncertain.
Second stage of maintenance infusion taper.
Final stage of maintenance infusion taper.

indications: Treatment of severe recurrent or prolonged seizures refractory to first-line therapies.

iv.instructions: Administer loading dose as an IV bolus over 10 minutes at a concentration ≤ 20 mg/mL. For continuous infusion, maintain concentration between 0.8 mg/mL and 8 mg/mL. Continuous EKG, heart rate, and blood pressure monitoring is required.
iv.preparation: Dilute 10% (100 mg/mL) and 20% (200 mg/mL) formulations prior to use. To prepare a 1 mg/mL bolus solution, dilute 0.5 mL of 2% lidocaine (10 mg) with 9.5 mL of NS or D5W. Only use preservative-free formulations without epinephrine.

contraindications: Severe degrees of sinoatrial, atrioventricular, or intraventricular block
Known hypersensitivity to amide-type local anesthetics

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of accelerated ventricular rate in patients with atrial fibrillation/flutter; increased risk of severe arrhythmias or complete heart block in patients with sinus bradycardia or incomplete heart block.
precautions.Hepatic: Increased risk of lidocaine toxicity due to impaired metabolic clearance.
precautions.Renal: Possible increased risk of toxicity in patients with impaired renal function.
precautions.Hematologic: Risk of methemoglobinemia, particularly in patients with G6PD deficiency, neonates < 6 months, or concurrent exposure to oxidizing agents.
precautions.Endocrine_Metabolic: Risk of malignant hyperthermia; requires immediate discontinuation and treatment.
precautions.Special_Populations: Viscous lidocaine is contraindicated for teething pain in infants/children due to risks of seizures, cardiopulmonary arrest, and death.

serious: Seizures and death (associated with pediatric oral use)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: The specific mechanism as an anticonvulsant remains unknown; acts as an amide-type local anesthetic.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Hepatic metabolism into active metabolites (MEGX and GX); approximately 30% excreted unchanged in urine.

Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Store at room temperature (20-25°C). Avoid excessive heat and protect from light.

admin_details.notes: Do not use in infant younger than 12 months who is receiving treatment with methemoglobin-inducing agents which include drugs in classes of nitrates/nitrites, local anesthetics, antineoplastic agents, antibiotics, antimalarials, anticonvulsants, and other drugs.

ranges.notes: Maximum application area of 10 cm2 for maximum application time of 1 hour.

indications: Local analgesia for normal intact skin
Local analgesia for genital mucous membranes in superficial minor surgery
Pretreatment for infiltration anesthesia
Analgesia for neonatal blood drawing and circumcision procedures

topical.instructions: Apply to intact skin or mucous membranes; for circumcision, apply 60 to 90 minutes before the procedure; avoid application near the eyes or middle ear; not recommended for heel lancing in neonates.
topical.preparation: Supplied in 5-g and 30-g tubes; typically used with an occlusive dressing (e.g., Tegaderm) to facilitate absorption.

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to any component of the product

precautions.Cardiovascular: Concomitant use with Class III anti-arrhythmic drugs (e.g., amiodarone, sotalol) may result in additive cardiac effects; monitoring is recommended.
precautions.Hepatic: Increased risk of toxic plasma concentrations in patients with severe hepatic disease.
precautions.Hematologic: Risk of methemoglobinemia, especially in infants <6 months, patients with G6PD deficiency, or those with cardiac/pulmonary compromise; risk increases with concurrent exposure to oxidizing agents.
precautions.Dermatologic: Risk of systemic toxicity increases with application to broken/irritated skin, covering large surface areas, or increased skin temperature.
precautions.Ophthalmic: Avoid eye contact; risk of severe irritation and loss of protective reflexes.
precautions.Otic: Potential for ototoxic effects if the drug migrates beyond the tympanic membrane into the middle ear.

serious: Life-threatening reactions due to excessive absorption

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Stabilizes neuronal membranes by inhibiting ionic fluxes required for the initiation and conduction of nerve impulses.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Hepatic metabolism to active metabolites followed by renal excretion.

availability: Each gram contains lidocaine 25 mg and prilocaine 25 mg (2.5%/2.5% eutectic mixture)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Preservative-free product; pH of 9.0; blood methemoglobin monitoring recommended if toxicity is suspected.

admin_details.notes: Supplied as ready-to-use infusion bags (2 mg/mL); no further dilution is necessary. Administer after hemodialysis. No adjustment for renal or mild-to-moderate hepatic impairment, though metabolites may accumulate in renal insufficiency.

ranges.notes: Dosing specifically for preterm newborns less than 1 week of age.
Usual maintenance dose for patients 1 week of age or older.

admin_details.notes: May administer without regard to timing of feedings. Before administering oral suspension, gently mix by inverting bottle 3 to 5 times. Do not shake.

ranges.notes: Dosing specifically for preterm newborns less than 1 week of age.
Usual maintenance dose for patients 1 week of age or older.

admin_details.notes: Supplied as ready-to-use infusion bags (2 mg/mL); no further dilution is necessary. Administer after hemodialysis.

ranges.notes: Used as IV triple therapy for systemic anthrax or IV combination therapy when meningitis is ruled out.
Used as IV triple therapy for systemic anthrax or IV combination therapy when meningitis is ruled out.
Used as IV triple therapy for systemic anthrax or IV combination therapy when meningitis is ruled out.

admin_details.notes: May administer without regard to timing of feedings. Before administering oral suspension, gently mix by inverting bottle 3 to 5 times. Do not shake.

ranges.notes: Usually oral therapy for antimicrobial course of prophylaxis from onset of illness.
Usually oral therapy for antimicrobial course of prophylaxis from onset of illness.
Usually oral therapy for antimicrobial course of prophylaxis from onset of illness.

indications: Treatment of refractory gram-positive infections including endocarditis and ventriculitis (e.g., MRSA, PRSP, and VRE).
Combination therapy for systemic anthrax in neonates (≥32 weeks gestational age) when meningitis is ruled out.
Triple IV therapy for systemic anthrax including meningitis or disseminated infection.
Oral follow-up therapy for severe anthrax infections.

iv.instructions: Administer as an intermittent IV infusion over 30 to 120 minutes.
iv.preparation: Supplied as ready-to-use 2 mg/mL infusion bags; no further dilution is required. Keep in foil overwrap until use.

oral.instructions: Administer without regard to timing of feedings. Gently mix suspension by inverting the bottle 3 to 5 times before each dose; do not shake.
oral.preparation: Reconstitute powder for suspension by adding 123 mL distilled water in two portions, shaking well between each portion, to achieve a final concentration of 100 mg/5 mL.

contraindications: Concomitant use of MAOIs or use within 2 weeks of MAOI therapy (e.g., phenelzine, isocarboxazid).

precautions.Cardiovascular: Use not recommended in uncontrolled hypertension, pheochromocytoma, or thyrotoxicosis unless monitored for blood pressure increases; risk of hypertension when used with sympathomimetics, vasopressors, or dopaminergic agents.
precautions.Hepatic: Liver dysfunction noted in pediatric cases of lactic acidosis; monitor lactate concentrations in patients with pre-existing hepatic impairment.
precautions.Renal: Monitor lactate concentrations in patients with pre-existing renal dysfunction; monitor for hyponatremia and SIADH.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Risk of Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD), ranging from mild diarrhea to fatal colitis.

serious: Serotonin syndrome (especially with concomitant serotonergic agents)
Peripheral and optic neuropathy (especially >28 days of use)
Increased mortality in patients with Gram-negative infections

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Oxazolidinone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis; primarily bacteriostatic but may be bactericidal against select organisms like S. pneumoniae.

pharmacokinetics.half_life: 2 to 3 hours in neonates; 5 to 6 hours in preterm neonates <1 week old
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Urine as unchanged drug (30%) and two inactive metabolites.

availability: 2 mg/mL ready-to-use flexible plastic infusion bags (100 mL, 300 mL)
100 mg/5 mL powder for oral suspension (150 mL after reconstitution)
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Reconstituted oral suspension is stable for 21 days at room temperature; IV bags should be protected from light and freezing.

admin_details.notes: Administer with a feeding. Use a calibrated dosing syringe. Oral solution contains ethanol (42.4% v/v) and propylene glycol (15.3% w/v). Oral solution not recommended for use with polyurethane feeding tubes; compatible with silicone and PVC. If coadministered with didanosine, give didanosine 1 hour before or 2 hours after.

ranges.notes: Dose reflects Lopinavir component (contains 4 mg/kg of Ritonavir). Once-daily dosing is NOT recommended. Use with caution in infants 14 days to 6 months due to alcohol/propylene glycol content. Avoid once-daily use with carbamazepine, efavirenz, nevirapine, nelfinavir, phenobarbital, or phenytoin.

Dose reflects Lopinavir component (contains 75 mg/m2 of Ritonavir). Once-daily dosing is NOT recommended. Use with caution in infants 14 days to 6 months due to alcohol/propylene glycol content. Avoid once-daily use with carbamazepine, efavirenz, nevirapine, nelfinavir, phenobarbital, or phenytoin.

indications: Preferred initial antiretroviral therapy for HIV-1 infection in neonates with postmenstrual age ≥42 weeks and postnatal age 14 days to <4 weeks.
Alternative regimen for HIV-1 infection in pediatric patients ≥4 weeks of age.
FDA-approved in combination with other antiretroviral agents for patients 14 days of age or older.

oral.instructions: Administer with a feeding to increase bioavailability. Use only the provided calibrated dosing syringe for the oral solution. If co-administered with didanosine, didanosine must be given 1 hour before or 2 hours after the lopinavir/ritonavir dose.
oral.preparation: Oral solution is incompatible with polyurethane feeding tubes due to ethanol and propylene glycol content; use only silicone or polyvinyl chloride (PVC) feeding tubes.

precautions.Cardiovascular: Risk of QT and PR interval prolongation, 2nd or 3rd-degree AV block, and Torsades de Pointes; avoid in patients with congenital long QT syndrome, hypokalemia, or structural heart disease.
precautions.Hepatic: Potential for new or worsening transaminase elevations and hepatic dysfunction, including fatalities; increased monitoring required for patients with Hepatitis B or C.
precautions.Renal: Risk of acute renal failure in preterm neonates due to propylene glycol accumulation.
precautions.Gastrointestinal: Pancreatitis has been reported, particularly in patients with significant hypertriglyceridemia or history of pancreatitis.

serious: Propylene glycol toxicity in neonates (lactic acidosis, CNS depression, respiratory complications)

Mechanism_Of_Action_Pharmacokinetics.mechanism_of_action: Lopinavir is an HIV protease inhibitor that prevents cleavage of the Gag-Pol polyprotein, resulting in immature, non-infectious viral particles. Ritonavir is included as a pharmacokinetic enhancer to inhibit CYP3A4 metabolism and increase lopinavir plasma concentrations.

pharmacokinetics.elimination: Feces (20% as unchanged drug), Urine (2% as unchanged drug)

availability: Oral solution: 80 mg lopinavir/20 mg ritonavir per mL
Special_Considerations_Preparation.stability: Refrigerated solution is stable until the expiration date. If stored at room temperature (up to 25°C/77°F), the solution must be used within 2 months.

admin_details.notes: Infuse at a rate not to exceed 2 mg per minute. Avoid intra-arterial administration and perivascular extravasation. While concentrations of 1 or 2 mg/mL can be used, 0.2 mg/mL or 0.4 mg/mL are more practical for neonates.

ranges.notes: Administer as a slow IV push. Repeat doses based on clinical response.

indications: Anticonvulsant for the acute management of seizures refractory to conventional therapy

iv.instructions: For intermittent use, infuse at a rate not exceeding 2 mg per minute. Strictly avoid intra-arterial administration and perivascular extravasation. Monitor respiratory status and injection site for phlebitis.
iv.preparation: Standard concentration: 1 or 2 mg/mL. Neonatal concentration: 0.2 mg/mL or 0.4 mg/mL.
